unit.name: Electronic Batch Record (EBR) System
unit.cfj: Compile and manage batch manufacturing and testing records in structured electronic format

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch is attested ready with critical deviations unresolved
rows.plain: Do not declare a batch ready when important problems are still unresolved. This helps prevent a product from being approved before serious concerns are addressed.
rows.imp_rat: Attesting readiness with critical deviations unresolved corrupts the disposition function entirely; consequences include releasing unsafe product and major regulatory action.
rows.sat_rat: Most EBR/deviation systems flag open records, but cross-linkage between deviation systems and the batch record is often imperfect, leaving real risk of a critical deviation slipping through. Deviation resolution is a known gate before disposition, but chasing open deviations manually adds slow back-and-forth before an overseer can trust attestation. Detecting that a critical deviation is still open depends heavily on the reviewer's diligence and product knowledge rather than any automatic block. An erroneous attestation with open critical deviations risks costly recalls, regulatory action, and rework, so the residual exposure is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production deviations or aseptic interventions omitted from each batch record
rows.plain: Record every production problem and every time workers must intervene in the sterile process. Complete records are needed to understand what happened to each batch.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted production deviations or aseptic interventions hide contamination risk central to sterile record completeness; catastrophic if unsafe product ships.
rows.sat_rat: Systems cannot detect events that were never entered, so unrecorded deviations and aseptic interventions remain a persistent, poorly served gap. Reconciling omitted deviations or interventions after the fact is slow and often reopens the record. Whether an intervention was documented depends on operator recognition and honesty in the moment, demanding sustained skilled attention the system cannot enforce. Omitted interventions surfaced late trigger costly investigations and possible batch rejection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic interventions omitted from the batch record
rows.plain: Record nearly every time workers must intervene in the sterile process. Missing these events makes it harder to judge whether the batch was made safely.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting aseptic interventions from the record hides contamination-relevant events; central to sterile completeness and catastrophic if unsafe product releases.
rows.sat_rat: Few EBRs actively detect that an unrecorded intervention occurred, so reliance on manual logging leaves omissions. Aseptic interventions happen under time pressure inside the isolator, so documentation is often deferred and sometimes forgotten. Capturing every intervention relies on operator memory and discipline during high-focus gowned work. Omitted interventions are severe aseptic-assurance gaps that can jeopardize sterility disposition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexpected sterile fill-finish conditions left unflagged during batch execution
rows.plain: Record and call attention to unexpected conditions during sterile production. Unreported problems can make it harder to protect the batch and investigate what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Unflagged unexpected sterile fill-finish conditions leave contamination risk unaddressed; central to sterile execution records and catastrophic in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: The EBR captures flagged events but does not detect unflagged ones, so reliability hinges on human observation. Flagging unexpected conditions in real time depends on operator noticing, with no time advantage from the record itself. Detecting unexpected fill-finish conditions relies heavily on trained operator judgement. Unflagged conditions can compromise sterility and force scrap, a costly outcome.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations or investigations remaining at disposition
rows.plain: Resolve as many production problems and investigations as possible before making the final decision about the batch. Unresolved issues can leave important safety questions unanswered.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations remaining at disposition directly defeat the readiness decision; catastrophic if unsafe product is released as a result.
rows.sat_rat: Interface gaps between EBR and QMS mean open deviations are not always automatically surfaced against the batch, so unresolved items can persist at disposition despite standard cross-checks. Deviation and investigation status is often tracked in a separate QMS, so reconciling open items to the batch can introduce waiting and manual cross-checking delays. Judging whether a deviation is genuinely closed versus nominally closed requires experienced QA interpretation that the system cannot automate. Manual linkage between EBR and deviation systems consumes QA labor and rework when linkages are incomplete.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of doubt about hidden sterility or product-risk concerns at disposition sign-off
rows.plain: Make sure the final approval decision is not clouded by unanswered concerns about hidden germs or other product risks. The decision should be supported by clear, trustworthy information.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about hidden sterility/product-risk at sign-off undermines confidence in the release decision; catastrophic if a concealed risk reaches patients.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical production deviation remains unflagged while affected sterile fill-finish processing continues
rows.plain: Make sure serious production problems are reported while the affected processing is still happening. This helps prevent more affected product from being made or used.
rows.imp_rat: An unflagged critical deviation while sterile processing continues is exactly what the MES must catch (top relevance), and the consequence is catastrophic — defective sterile product may reach patients undetected.
rows.sat_rat: MES flags configured deviations reliably but many real-world deviations fall outside coded rules, leaving meaningful recurring gaps. Real-time deviation flagging can halt or hold processing quickly once a rule is triggered, but detection of subtle deviations still lags. Automated rule-based flagging reduces dependence on an operator noticing a drift, though rule design requires expertise. Catching deviations before continued processing avoids expensive downstream batch loss, justifying the configuration cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a sterility-critical condition excursion during conformance confirmation
rows.plain: Make it less likely that someone misses a condition that could make the product no longer germ-free while checking that instructions were followed. This helps prevent unsafe product from being accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a sterility-critical excursion during conformance confirmation defeats the safety check; central and catastrophic in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Unless environmental systems are integrated with limit alarms, overlooking a critical excursion remains a real risk. Excursion review can be near-real-time with alarms, though manual confirmation still takes operator attention. Recognizing sterility-critical excursions demands significant operator and reviewer expertise beyond what the EBR supplies. An overlooked sterility excursion can cost an entire batch or worse, a very high-stakes failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterility-assurance records missing at readiness review
rows.plain: Make sure all records needed to show that the product is safe and clean are available when readiness is reviewed. This helps prevent a batch from being approved without enough evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterility-assurance records at readiness review directly obstruct the sterile-release decision; catastrophic if a batch clears without sterility evidence.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-system dependencies mean required sterility-assurance records are frequently missing or unlinked at review despite checklists. Sterility-assurance records often arrive from separate lab/environmental systems, so waiting on and linking them slows readiness review. Knowing the full set of required sterility records demands specialist knowledge not always encoded in the checklist. Missing sterility records at review force delays and re-review, an expensive interruption near release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic interventions lacking complete documentation at readiness review
rows.plain: Record all times when workers had to interfere with the clean production process before readiness is reviewed. This helps show what happened and whether the product remained safe.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic interventions lacking complete documentation at readiness leave contamination risk unassessed; central to sterile release and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Because interventions are documented manually in the moment, incomplete records surfacing at readiness review remain common and hard to prevent. Completing intervention documentation retroactively at review is slow and disruptive. Complete aseptic-intervention documentation relies on operator judgement during the fill, a highly skill-dependent and error-prone task. Incomplete intervention documentation risks batch rejection of high-value sterile product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compiled batch records containing entries that conflict with actual production execution
rows.plain: Reduce the number of completed production records that do not match what actually happened. Accurate records help show that the work was done correctly and can withstand an inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Entries conflicting with actual production execution is a falsity defect central to a truthful record with severe risk to release integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Systems record what was entered, not what actually happened, so conflicts between record and reality are a recurring, weakly-served risk. Detecting entries that conflict with actual execution requires slow investigative comparison. Spotting conflicts between recorded and actual production demands expert scrutiny the system rarely automates. Records conflicting with execution can void a batch, an expensive outcome.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination during a required sterile fill-finish quality check
rows.plain: Make sure the required product-quality check does not introduce germs into the product or the areas that touch it. This helps keep the product safe during testing.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during a sterile fill-finish quality check is central to the MES's job of controlling critical production activities (relevance high), and severity is catastrophic since contaminated sterile product can cause patient harm and full batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: MES can prompt and log the check but the contamination itself is a physical/environmental outcome largely outside software control, so this need is poorly served. MES sequencing enforces aseptic-check timing but does not shorten the physical intervention window during which contamination risk actually arises. Preventing contamination during a sterile fill-finish check still depends heavily on operator aseptic technique that MES software cannot directly govern. A contamination event triggers costly batch loss and investigation, and status-quo MES does little to avert the physical root cause.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of batch release despite unresolved sterility-assurance failures
rows.plain: Do not release a batch while important cleanliness problems are still unresolved. Releasing it too soon could send an unsafe product onward.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a batch despite unresolved sterility-assurance failures directly defeats the EBR's job of certifying record-based readiness; consequences are catastrophic patient harm and recall.
rows.sat_rat: Configured EBR gating plus mandatory review substantially reduces slip-through, yet nuanced sterility-assurance signals (environmental excursions, borderline results) still occasionally evade automated logic requiring vigilant human catch. EBR systems flag outstanding sterility-related results and hold batches until entered, avoiding premature timing-driven release for this need. Recognizing that a sterility-assurance failure is truly unresolved still relies heavily on microbiological expertise and QA judgement that automated checks cannot fully replace. The system cheaply enforces mandatory sterility checkpoints, but the cost of a missed failure is catastrophic, so residual manual review remains resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical threshold breach fails to generate an alert during sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: Make sure the system gives a warning whenever an important safety limit is exceeded during medicine production. A timely warning lets workers act before the process becomes unsafe or the medicine is affected.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to alert on a critical threshold breach during sterile fill-finish directly negates the MES's measure-and-control job (top relevance) and is catastrophic since undetected excursions compromise sterile product safety.
rows.sat_rat: Alarming on defined thresholds is mature, but missed alerts still occur through mis-configured tags, suppressed alarms, or integration failures. Threshold-breach alerting is near-instantaneous when tags are monitored live. Automated alerting removes reliance on human watch-standing of critical parameters. Alert infrastructure is a standard cost item and prevents costly undetected excursions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of atypical results omitted from the batch record
rows.plain: Record all unusual results in the batch record instead of leaving some out. Complete records help people understand whether anything went wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting atypical results from the record hides potential failures from disposition; central to complete evidence and catastrophic if failing product releases.
rows.sat_rat: There is limited system enforcement forcing atypical results into the record, so omissions remain a real risk. Prompted atypical-result flags surface omissions early rather than at review. Recognizing and choosing to record an atypical result depends on analyst judgement. Omitted atypical results are a serious compliance cost if discovered later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material-quantity, container-count, or yield discrepancies left unreconciled at readiness review
rows.plain: Make sure differences in ingredient amounts, container counts, or total product made are explained before deciding that the batch is ready.
rows.imp_rat: Unreconciled material/container/yield discrepancies at readiness signal potential mix-ups central to release integrity with severe safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: EBR calculations help flag arithmetic mismatches, but true reconciliation against physical reality still leaves discrepancies open at review. Reconciling quantity and yield discrepancies at review is a slow manual investigation. Yield and container reconciliation requires skilled interpretation of multiple data sources rather than a simple system check. Unreconciled material discrepancies can indicate loss or diversion, with costly investigation and rejection consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations or record discrepancies in batch records presented for inspection
rows.plain: Make sure batch records contain no unexplained problems or conflicting entries when inspectors review them.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations/discrepancies in records presented for inspection risk regulatory findings and release of flawed batches; central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems link to batches but do not guarantee all are resolved before an inspection package is assembled. Purging unresolved deviations before inspection is a rushed, time-consuming scramble. Judging inspection-readiness of deviation records requires seasoned quality expertise. Unresolved deviations found in inspection risk findings and remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among manufacturing records, laboratory results, and investigation findings at disposition
rows.plain: Make sure the production records, test results, and investigation findings agree before making the final decision about the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among manufacturing, lab, and investigation records at disposition undermines the coherent evidence base needed to decide; severe risk of wrong release.
rows.sat_rat: Because EBR, LIMS, and investigation systems are frequently poorly integrated, contradictory data can coexist unnoticed until an experienced reviewer spots it, leaving this need only modestly served. Reconciling manufacturing records against lab results and investigation findings is often a manual, time-consuming comparison across disparate systems. Detecting subtle conflicts among data sources demands significant reviewer expertise and contextual knowledge, as the system rarely cross-validates semantically. The manual reconciliation effort across LIMS, EBR, and QMS adds meaningful review cost and rework when discrepancies emerge late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of disposition decisions lacking traceable batch evidence and documented rationale
rows.plain: Make sure each decision about what to do with a batch is supported by records that can be followed back to that batch and by a written explanation. This helps show that the decision was sound.
rows.imp_rat: Disposition decisions lacking traceable evidence and rationale defeat the EBR's core purpose of evidence-based release; catastrophic and central.
rows.sat_rat: EBR disposition workflows enforce documented rationale and link evidence reasonably well, though rationale quality still varies with the individual approver. Electronic evidence linking and templated approval steps make traceable rationale capture faster than assembling paper trails. Structured disposition workflows guide reviewers to attach evidence and rationale, reducing dependence on individual diligence. Systematized traceability lowers the cost of reconstructing decision basis during audits and inspections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrectly configured thresholds allow critical deviations to remain unflagged
rows.plain: Set the warning limits correctly so serious problems are detected and reported. Incorrect limits could allow important problems to go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Misconfigured thresholds letting deviations go unflagged attacks the MES's detection role (high relevance) and is severe because silent excursions in sterile production can harm patients.
rows.sat_rat: Misconfigured thresholds are a common latent defect because verification of every limit is inconsistent and often incomplete. Defining and testing every threshold across parameters is a lengthy manual effort during preparation. Setting correct limits demands deep process knowledge that is scarce and error-prone to capture. Threshold definition and revalidation consume engineering hours but no major material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between an aseptic intervention entry and the observed event
rows.plain: Make sure the written record of a hands-on change or interruption matches what actually happened. This helps keep the work history accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between an aseptic intervention entry and the observed event misrepresents contamination-relevant facts; central to sterile records and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Free-text intervention entries are prone to diverging from the actual event with little system check. Recording under aseptic pressure often delays entry, widening the gap to memory-based detail. Accurately describing the observed intervention depends heavily on operator recall and expertise. Discrepant intervention records undermine aseptic assurance and trigger costly review.

rows.stk: MES system administrators, IT operations staff
rows.source_job: Monitor MES application health and logs
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical MES anomaly fails to produce an actionable alert
rows.plain: Make sure a serious problem in the production computer system creates a warning that someone can act on. Otherwise, an important problem could go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: A critical anomaly failing to produce an actionable alert directly negates the MES's monitoring purpose (high relevance), and severity is high because unaddressed anomalies can escalate to batch loss or safety events.
rows.sat_rat: Monitoring covers known failure modes well, but novel or unmonitored anomalies still occasionally fail to raise an actionable alert. Alerting fires quickly once configured, so detection latency is generally low. Deciding which anomalies warrant an actionable alert requires experienced engineers to tune thresholds and avoid alert fatigue. Alerting infrastructure is standard, but tuning and maintaining coverage consumes recurring effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an MES workflow allowing production to proceed without required quality authorization
rows.plain: Prevent the production process from continuing unless the required quality approval has been given. This ensures that unapproved work does not move forward.
rows.imp_rat: Production proceeding without required quality authorization is a fundamental breach of the MES gating function (top relevance) and is severe because unauthorized product can bypass safety checks.
rows.sat_rat: MES workflow engines can enforce interlocks, yet gaps in the configured logic that let production bypass authorization still recur. Building quality-hold gates into workflows lengthens configuration and testing timelines. Encoding mandatory quality authorizations into workflow logic requires experienced process/quality engineers. Workflow modeling and validation effort is a significant but planned expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of disposition based on incomplete manufacturing or testing records
rows.plain: Do not make a final decision about a batch when important production or test records are missing. Complete records are needed to judge whether the batch is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Disposition on incomplete records defeats the record-based decision the EBR exists to support; consequences are catastrophic release of unverified product.
rows.sat_rat: This is among the best-served needs because EBR systems structurally block disposition on records with open mandatory fields or missing entries, making incomplete-record dispositions rare where the system is properly configured. Electronic completeness checks run continuously and near-instantly, preventing the delays of manual page-by-page completeness verification. Mandatory-field logic and exception reporting offload much of the completeness scrutiny from the reviewer, lowering the expertise burden. Automated completeness enforcement avoids the labor cost of exhaustive manual reconciliation of every record before disposition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material-usage records containing an incorrect material identity
rows.plain: Have fewer material-use records that name the wrong material. Correct identities help show that the right materials went into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect material identity in usage records can indicate a mix-up; central to material accountability and severe for product safety.
rows.sat_rat: Scan-verified material identity is fairly reliable, but manual fallbacks still permit errors. Scanning material identity is fast where integrated; manual entry adds time. Barcode verification reduces reliance on operator recognizing correct material identity. Wrong material identity is a serious quality risk with high remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prescribed testing steps omitted from an analysis
rows.plain: Leave out fewer of the testing steps that are required for an analysis. Following every step helps make the results complete and dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Prescribed testing steps omitted from an analysis means the product is untested against a requirement; central and severe for release safety.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced sequencing catches many omissions, but steps done off-system or overridden still get skipped. Step-by-step electronic execution keeps pace with testing without added delay. Guided workflows reduce reliance on the analyst remembering every prescribed step. An omitted step can invalidate a test and force repeat work, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of proceeding with batch execution while a procedural deviation remains unresolved
rows.plain: Do not continue making a batch while a mistake or departure from the instructions is still unresolved. The issue should be understood and addressed before work continues.
rows.imp_rat: Proceeding while a procedural deviation is unresolved compounds nonconformance during execution; central to record-driven control and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Configured stop-gates on open deviations make unauthorized continuation fairly uncommon, though not all deviations are system-tracked. Deviation flags can hold the step immediately, preventing continuation without delay to detection. Workflow gating reduces the judgement operators need about whether to proceed with an open deviation. Proceeding on an unresolved deviation risks batch loss, and holds prevent much of this cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a compiled record contains content from the wrong batch or master-record version
rows.plain: Make sure the completed batch record contains information only from the correct batch and the correct approved instructions. Information from another batch or an older version could make the record misleading.
rows.imp_rat: Content from the wrong batch or master version corrupts record integrity centrally, causing mislabeled evidence and potential wrong-product release.
rows.sat_rat: EBR systems generally bind entries to a specific batch instance and master version, making wrong-batch content relatively rare though not impossible via manual attachments. Automated batch/lot binding avoids time-consuming manual cross-checking of content provenance. System context (batch ID, master version) reduces reliance on operators to correctly associate content. Cross-contamination of record content is costly if it reaches disposition, but tight electronic binding keeps incidence low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded values differing from the completed test output
rows.plain: Reduce the number of values entered into the records that do not match the completed test. This helps keep the records accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values differing from actual test output is a data-integrity failure central to the compilation job, with severe risk of releasing off-spec product.
rows.sat_rat: Instrument interfacing and required-field validation in modern EBRs meaningfully reduce value mismatches, but manual transcription paths still let discrepancies through. EBR entry happens close to test execution, so transcription lag is limited but manual keying still slows verification of match to the instrument output. Correct recording still depends on the analyst's care and attention when reading and typing values, a persistent skill/vigilance burden. Mismatch errors trigger costly investigations and reprocessing, though EBR validation catches some before they propagate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying obsolete or incorrect acceptance criteria to batch evidence
rows.plain: The current and correct rules for judging product evidence should be used. This helps ensure the release decision is based on the right requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Applying obsolete acceptance criteria can pass genuinely failing product; central to correct evaluation and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Where specifications are managed and version-controlled in the EBR they are applied consistently, but manual spec updates and interface lags can still allow obsolete criteria in some configurations. Version-controlled specifications embedded in the EBR make current acceptance criteria immediately available without manual lookup delay. Automated specification linking reduces reliance on the reviewer remembering or locating the correct current spec. Electronic spec management avoids the cost of errors and rework from applying superseded criteria pulled from paper documents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of preliminary or invalid analytical results represented as final
rows.plain: Reduce the number of early or invalid test results that are mistakenly shown as final results. This helps ensure decisions are based only on trustworthy results.
rows.imp_rat: Preliminary or invalid results represented as final can pass off-spec product; central to result integrity with severe release consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Status handling reduces but does not eliminate invalid results appearing as final, especially across repeat testing. Marking results invalid is fast but reconciling which is final can delay closure. Judging which preliminary results are invalid requires analyst and reviewer expertise. Presenting invalid results as final risks costly release errors and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attesting a manufacturing step before the assigned work is complete
rows.plain: A worker should not confirm that a manufacturing step is finished until all assigned work is actually complete. This keeps the records accurate and prevents unfinished work from being treated as done.
rows.imp_rat: Attesting a step before work is complete falsifies completion status central to record truthfulness with serious downstream release risk.
rows.sat_rat: The system cannot verify actual work completion, so premature attestation remains a behavioral risk poorly controlled. Attestation is prompted at step end, but nothing enforces that physical work actually finished first. Preventing premature attestation depends entirely on operator honesty and diligence. Early attestation is a data-integrity violation with high remediation cost if discovered.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compiled analytical values differing from executed test results
rows.plain: Make sure the numbers copied into the final analysis match the results actually found during testing. This prevents decisions from being based on incorrect information.
rows.imp_rat: Compiled analytical values differing from executed results is a core data-integrity defect that can lead to releasing failing product—both central and severe.
rows.sat_rat: System interfaces and locked calculations make many compiled values match executed results reliably, yet manual transfers remain a recurring failure point. Compilation into the EBR is generally contemporaneous with testing, limiting delay-induced drift. Where automated data capture exists the skill dependence is low, but manual compilation still relies on analyst accuracy. Compilation discrepancies drive rework and OOS-style investigations that consume analyst and QA time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recipe parameters being configured outside approved production limits
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that the production recipe is set outside the approved limits. This helps prevent the equipment from making batches incorrectly or unsafely.
rows.imp_rat: Recipe parameters configured outside approved limits undermines the MES's control of production quality (high relevance) and is severe since bad recipes systematically produce defective batches.
rows.sat_rat: Built-in range checks and approval workflows are mature and catch most out-of-limit entries, though gaps in configured limits still let some through. Configured limit checks operate in real time so they rarely add delay to recipe setup. Defining approved limits still requires process-expert judgement, but once encoded the system enforces them without expert attention. The enforcement logic is largely built into the MES, so ongoing cost is low once configured.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch record is compiled against the wrong approved master version
rows.plain: Make sure the production record is prepared using the correct, currently approved master instructions. This prevents the record from being based on an outdated or wrong version.
rows.imp_rat: Compiling against the wrong approved master version invalidates the entire record foundation; severe because the batch is effectively made to unapproved specs.
rows.sat_rat: Modern EBR platforms with enforced effective-dated master templates make wrong-version compilation uncommon, a relative strength of the status quo. Version-controlled master issuance is largely automated, so little time is spent verifying the correct version. System-enforced master selection removes most of the judgement previously needed to pick the right approved version. Wrong-version compilation would be very costly, but controlled issuance makes this rare enough that cost exposure is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unattributed changes in compiled batch records
rows.plain: Make sure every change in the completed batch record can be traced to the person or reason behind it. This supports accurate and trustworthy records.
rows.imp_rat: Unattributed changes in compiled records is a fundamental data-integrity violation; central to trustworthy records and severe for regulatory standing.
rows.sat_rat: EBR audit trails enforce attributed changes almost universally, making unattributed changes rare — a relative strength. Automatic change attribution needs no extra reviewer time. Enforced e-signature on changes removes reliance on manual annotation. Reliable attribution avoids costly data-integrity investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of MES shutdown interrupting active sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: Prevent the production-control system from shutting down while sterile filling and finishing work is still underway. This avoids interrupting an active production run.
rows.imp_rat: An MES shutdown interrupting active sterile fill-finish directly defeats the system's job of controlling production (very high relevance), and the consequence is severe — lost batches, deviations, and potential compromised sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice retires MES only after production is idled and uses staged cutovers, so a shutdown hitting a live sterile batch is rare but the consequences when interlocks are misjudged remain severe. Coordinating a shutdown window around active sterile fill-finish runs requires scheduling into validated production gaps, and today that timing is manually negotiated with limited slack. Avoiding an interruption depends on experienced automation and validation engineers who understand the MES-to-line couplings, and such staff are scarce during decommissioning projects. An accidental interruption of a sterile batch can scrap high-value product and trigger costly investigations, so the stakes are extreme even if the mitigation spend is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of atypical results documented with a value differing from the observed value
rows.plain: Record unusual results exactly as they were observed, so the written record does not give a different value. This helps people trust the test results.
rows.imp_rat: Atypical results documented with a value differing from observed is data falsification; central to result integrity with severe integrity and safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Structured fields help, but atypical documentation often relies on manual entry that can diverge from the observed value. Documenting atypical results in the moment is quick if fields exist, slower if free-text. Accurately capturing the observed atypical value demands analyst attentiveness. A misrecorded atypical value can misdirect an investigation at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of atypical results linked to the wrong batch, sample, or test
rows.plain: Make sure each unusual result is connected to the correct batch, sample, or test. This prevents findings from being assigned to the wrong work.
rows.imp_rat: Atypical results linked to the wrong batch/sample/test misdirect critical failure evidence; central to traceability and severe for release decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Guided linking reduces errors, but atypical documentation often uses manual paths with weaker association controls. Linking atypical results at capture avoids later reattachment time. Correctly associating an atypical result depends on analyst judgement. A mislinked atypical result can derail an investigation at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material, yield, label, or container discrepancies remaining at disposition
rows.plain: Resolve differences in material amounts, production results, labels, or containers before making the final decision about the batch. This helps ensure the final decision is based on complete and accurate information.
rows.imp_rat: Material, yield, label, or container discrepancies at disposition signal reconciliation failures central to release integrity with severe safety/mix-up risk.
rows.sat_rat: Material, yield, and container reconciliations are well handled by structured EBR logic, though label and manual-count discrepancies still occasionally slip through where data is keyed manually. Automated yield and material reconciliation calculations run quickly within the EBR, reducing manual tally time. Built-in reconciliation formulas and tolerance checks lower the arithmetic and vigilance burden on the reviewer. System-enforced reconciliation reduces wasted investigation effort chasing discrepancies that would otherwise surface late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material-usage records containing an incorrect consumed quantity
rows.plain: Record the amount of material actually used, without entering a wrong quantity. Accurate amounts help show what went into the product and what remains.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect consumed quantity in material-usage records corrupts material reconciliation central to the record with serious accountability consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Range checks catch gross errors but plausible mis-keyed quantities frequently slip through. Quantity entry is manual and quick, but verification adds little assurance in real time. Correct quantity still depends on the operator reading and typing accurately. Wrong quantities break reconciliation and yield, causing costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of executed steps whose recorded data do not match the line activity
rows.plain: Make sure the information recorded for each completed step matches what workers actually did on the production line. This helps show that the work followed the instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded data not matching line activity is a fidelity defect central to a truthful batch record; can mask real process problems, raising severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without instrument integration, recorded data can diverge from actual line activity, and range checks catch only implausible values. Manual keying still takes time and transcription happens after observing the line, allowing drift between activity and record. Correct entry still depends heavily on operator attention to read instruments and type values accurately. Data mismatches drive deviations and reprocessing, and status quo does little to prevent honest transcription error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required manufacturing, intervention, environmental-monitoring, and testing data omitted from the batch-record structure
rows.plain: Include all required production, interruption, surrounding-condition, and testing information in the batch record. Complete records reduce the risk of missing information needed to show that the product was made properly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required manufacturing/EM/testing data from the structure creates broad completeness gaps central to compilation with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Broad data coverage is partly standardized but environmental-monitoring and intervention data are frequently underspecified. Cataloguing all required data streams into the structure is a lengthy specification effort. Comprehensive data-scope definition needs expertise spanning manufacturing, EM, and testing domains. Missing data categories cause rework and potential batch documentation gaps that are costly to fix.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recorded analytical data is lost during compilation
rows.plain: Make sure recorded test results are not lost while they are being gathered into one report. This preserves the information needed to review the work and make decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of recorded analytical data during compilation destroys the evidence the job produces; severe as it may be irrecoverable and block disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Autosave, transactional writes and audit trails in EBR systems make outright loss of recorded data uncommon. Electronic saving is near-instant, so little time is lost preserving data compared to paper. Persisting data requires little skill once the system autosaves; the burden is minimal. Data loss during compilation is rare and largely prevented, avoiding costly re-testing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of adjusting a setpoint outside validated operating limits during active production
rows.plain: Prevent the operator from changing a process setting beyond the safe, tested range while production is running. This helps keep the product and equipment within known safe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Adjusting a setpoint outside validated limits during active production strikes at the MES's control mandate (high relevance), and severity is high since out-of-spec conditions can ruin batches and endanger product quality.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-parameter limit enforcement is established practice and works well, but coverage gaps remain where setpoints live in equipment layers outside MES. Validated-limit range checks fire instantly at the moment of setpoint entry with no operational delay. Hard-coded operating envelopes reduce reliance on the operator knowing every validated limit from memory. Blocking out-of-limit setpoints prevents scrap and deviation costs, though configuration and validation of limits carry effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-record exceptions with unclear resolution status at readiness review
rows.plain: Have fewer issues in the production record with no clear answer when someone checks whether the production run is ready. This makes the readiness decision clearer and safer.
rows.imp_rat: Exceptions with unclear resolution status at readiness obscure whether risks are closed; central to the readiness decision with serious release-risk impact.
rows.sat_rat: Status fields in tracking systems help, but inconsistent updating leaves exception status unclear at review fairly often. Clarifying exception status at review adds delay when statuses are ambiguous. Interpreting unclear resolution status needs experienced reviewers. Ambiguous status causes rework and re-review, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory manufacturing or testing evidence elements omitted from the specified record structure
rows.plain: Leave out fewer required pieces of proof about manufacturing or testing from the record format. This makes it easier to show inspectors that the work was done properly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting mandatory evidence elements from the record structure creates systemic completeness gaps central to the compile job with serious downstream impact.
rows.sat_rat: Established GMP templates cover common elements but exception scenarios still get omitted with meaningful frequency. Checklists speed identification of mandatory evidence elements but assembling them remains time-consuming. Knowing which evidence elements are mandatory requires solid GMP expertise that experienced staff usually possess. Omissions found in audits force expensive remediation of record structures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of altered, duplicated, unauthenticated, or unexplained entries used as disposition evidence
rows.plain: Use fewer records that were changed, copied, not proven genuine, or have no clear explanation when deciding whether a batch can be released. Decisions should rely on trustworthy evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Altered, duplicated, or unauthenticated entries used as disposition evidence is a data-integrity fraud-level failure; catastrophic for compliance and patient safety.
rows.sat_rat: This is the best-served need since Part -compliant EBR systems inherently prevent unauthenticated or silently altered entries and log all changes, making unexplained disposition evidence rare. Audit trails and electronic signatures are captured automatically in real time with no added reviewer effort. System-enforced authentication and audit trails remove the need for reviewers to manually verify data authenticity or handwriting. Automated integrity controls avoid the substantial cost of forensic data-integrity investigations for authenticated electronic entries.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a compiled batch record cannot be retrieved when requested during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure the complete production record can be found quickly when an inspector asks for it. Missing records can make it impossible to show what happened during production.
rows.imp_rat: A compiled record that cannot be retrieved during inspection fails a core obligation of the EBR; central to record management and severe for compliance standing.
rows.sat_rat: Validated EBR systems reliably retrieve stored records on demand, making non-retrieval rare — a clear status-quo strength. Electronic retrieval is near-instant during inspection. Search and indexing remove reliance on staff memory of where records reside. Fast retrieval avoids costly inspection delays and findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the original observation is overwritten during atypical-result documentation
rows.plain: Keep the first observation unchanged when recording an unusual result. The original reading or finding is important for understanding what actually happened.
rows.imp_rat: Overwriting the original observation during atypical documentation destroys audit trail integrity; central to data integrity and severe for inspection defensibility.
rows.sat_rat: Immutable audit trails largely prevent true overwrites, though displayed values can still obscure the original if not reviewed. Audit-trailed edits preserve originals without extra time from the analyst. The system retains prior values automatically, reducing reliance on analyst care. Overwriting an original observation can undermine an investigation at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recording a consumed quantity that exceeds the quantity issued or available
rows.plain: Prevent the recorded amount used from being greater than the amount that was handed out or was actually available. This keeps material records accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recording a consumed quantity exceeding what was issued signals a reconciliation or mix-up error central to material integrity with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Only some EBRs enforce issued-versus-consumed checks, so this remains partially controlled. Real-time balance checks against issued quantity flag over-consumption instantly where integrated. System reconciliation reduces the operator's need to mentally track available quantities. Recording impossible consumption corrupts reconciliation and causes costly rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conditional manufacturing steps skipped without documented justification in each batch record
rows.plain: Reduce the number of required production steps that are skipped without a written reason in the batch record. Complete records make it clear what was done and why.
rows.imp_rat: Skipped conditional steps without documented justification create unexplained gaps central to record completeness with serious conformance risk.
rows.sat_rat: EBR conditional logic enforces many branch paths, but justification capture for legitimately skipped steps remains inconsistent. Conditional-step logic in EBR reduces time otherwise spent verifying branch completion. Guided conditional workflows reduce reliance on operators to know which steps apply. Skipped conditional steps without justification risk rework, but branching logic limits incidence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required observed measurements omitted from the batch record
rows.plain: Record all measurements that must be watched during production. Missing measurements can make it difficult to tell whether the product was made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required observed measurements creates gaps central to a complete manufacturing record with serious consequences for verification.
rows.sat_rat: Templated observation fields catch most omissions, yet observations not built into the form can still be left out. Prompted fields surface missing observations at entry rather than during later review. Structured prompts lessen dependence on analyst memory for which observations to record. Missing observations found at review trigger rework, but the frequency is moderated by templates.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic intervention entries missing required details
rows.plain: Make sure every record of entering or handling the sterile work area includes all required details. Complete records help show that the work was done safely.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic intervention entries missing required details limit contamination risk assessment; central to sterile records with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Required-field enforcement improves completeness, but free-text detail quality still varies. Structured intervention forms speed entry but detail completion competes with in-process aseptic demands. Mandatory-field prompts reduce reliance on operator recall of what details to capture. Incomplete intervention records trigger investigations that are expensive and time-consuming.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of critical batch-execution workflows malfunctioning after the MES version update
rows.plain: Make sure essential production steps still work correctly after updating the software that manages production records and processes. This helps prevent errors or interruptions during a batch.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-execution workflows malfunctioning after a version update disables the MES's primary control function (high relevance), and severity is high due to disrupted production and potential batch integrity loss.
rows.sat_rat: Formal validation and staged rollouts in regulated plants catch many defects, but subtle workflow malfunctions still slip through into production periodically. Regression testing before a version update takes significant time, and rushed windows often truncate the coverage that would catch batch-workflow breakage. Validating batch-execution workflows post-update demands scarce staff who deeply understand both the MES and the production process. Comprehensive validation environments and test cycles are expensive, so many sites under-invest and accept residual risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required sterile fill-finish genealogy events omitted from MES traceability configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the electronic tracking system records every required step in making and finishing the product. Complete records make it possible to trace what happened to each product.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted genealogy events in traceability configuration weakens the MES's tracking function (moderately high relevance) and is severe for recalls and investigations, though it is a configuration completeness issue rather than an active-production stop.
rows.sat_rat: Omitted genealogy events are a chronic gap because completeness is hard to verify until an audit or recall exposes it. Mapping every genealogy event for sterile fill-finish is painstaking and slow to configure. Accurately modeling aseptic genealogy requires rare specialists combining GMP and MES expertise. The specialist labor and validation for full traceability is expensive but justified by regulation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restricted units, lots, or tasks progress despite an active MES hold
rows.plain: Prevent equipment, batches, or jobs that have been put on hold from moving forward. This ensures that production stops until the hold is cleared.
rows.imp_rat: Restricted units progressing despite an active hold directly bypasses a core MES control (high relevance), and severity is high since held product moving forward risks releasing defective sterile material.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-and-restrict enforcement is a well-established, dependable MES control though edge cases in partial-lot or cross-system holds persist. Active holds block progression the instant they are applied with no delay. System-enforced holds remove reliance on staff remembering which units are restricted. Preventing held material from advancing avoids costly quarantine breaches and recalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic interventions linked to the wrong batch or manufacturing step
rows.plain: Reduce the share of hands-on actions taken to protect cleanliness that are recorded against the wrong product group or work step. This helps keep the production record accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic interventions linked to the wrong batch/step misattributes contamination risk; central to sterile traceability and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Session-locked context makes wrong batch/step linkage of interventions fairly uncommon. Batch/step context is preset, so linking interventions correctly costs no extra time. Contextual linking reduces the operator's need to manually select the correct batch and step. Mislinked interventions cause traceability gaps that are costly to investigate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a provisional result is recorded as final
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an early test result is recorded as the confirmed final result. This helps ensure decisions are based on the correct result.
rows.imp_rat: A provisional result recorded as final can drive disposition on non-confirmed data; central to result integrity and serious in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Draft/final status controls exist but are inconsistently enforced, leaving real risk of provisional values passing as final. Status flags for provisional versus final are quick to set but easy to overlook. Distinguishing provisional from final still leans on analyst discipline and understanding. A provisional value released as final can cause costly wrong dispositions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between documented conditions and actual execution conditions
rows.plain: Make the written description of the test conditions match what actually happened as closely as possible. Accurate records help others understand and trust the test results.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between documented and actual execution conditions falsifies the record's context; central to fidelity and serious for result validity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual condition entry allows discrepancies between documented and actual conditions when not instrument-fed. Real-time entry limits drift between actual and documented conditions. Accurately transcribing conditions depends on analyst attentiveness. Divergent condition records undermine reviews and can cause costly re-work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a recorded result is changed without attributable documentation
rows.plain: Do not change a recorded test result without documenting who changed it and why. This keeps the record trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: A result changed without attributable documentation is a core data-integrity violation; central to trustworthy records and severe for compliance standing.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory audit trails are a mature, strong EBR control that reliably captures who changed what and why. Audit trails record changes automatically with no analyst time cost. No special skill is needed for the system to attribute and timestamp a change. Attributable change history prevents costly disputes over what was altered.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that MES production records fail to persist in the provisioned database
rows.plain: Make sure production records are saved reliably in the database set up to hold them.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to persist production records undermines the MES's core measurement/record function (high relevance) and is severe because lost regulated records can invalidate batches and trigger compliance failures.
rows.sat_rat: ACID-compliant databases with write-ahead logging and replication make lost production records rare when correctly configured. Provisioning a resilient database with commit-confirmation is a standard, quick step in modern MES deployment scripts. DBAs and infrastructure engineers routinely configure transactional persistence, so the skill exists broadly. Enterprise-grade databases and redundant storage add cost but are budgeted as baseline infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an operator without current qualification is assigned to a sterile-area task
rows.plain: Make sure only workers with up-to-date training and approval are assigned tasks in the sterile area.
rows.imp_rat: Enforcing operator qualification for sterile-area tasks is a core control the MES exists to measure and enforce (high relevance), and assigning an unqualified operator to a sterile task carries severe compliance and patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Training/LMS-integrated MES gating is a mature, widely deployed control that reliably blocks unqualified operators when configured correctly. Role- and qualification-based access checks in mature MES gate task assignment in real time with negligible delay. The system encodes the qualification matrix so it no longer relies on a supervisor remembering who is currently certified, directly removing an attention-dependent error. Automated qualification enforcement avoids rework and regulatory findings that would otherwise cost far more than the licensing overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required analytical data points omitted from the batch record
rows.plain: Make sure all required laboratory measurements are included in the production record. Missing results can make it impossible to confirm that the batch met its requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required analytical data points leaves the record incomplete, undermining the compile job and risking disposition without full evidence.
rows.sat_rat: Configured mandatory fields reliably prevent many omissions, though data points outside the template can still be missed. Required-field enforcement flags omissions immediately rather than at review, saving downstream time. Mandatory-field logic reduces reliance on the analyst remembering every required data point. Omitted data points caught late cause costly delays, but early flagging limits this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between the corrected value and the authoritative analytical result
rows.plain: Make the corrected test entry match the trusted test result as closely as possible. This keeps the records accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between a corrected value and the authoritative result means the record misstates the true outcome; central and serious for disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Audit-trailed corrections reduce silent divergence, but the corrected value can still differ from the true result if entered wrongly. Correction workflows add controlled time but keep the authoritative value traceable. Determining the correct authoritative value during correction still requires analyst judgement. Discrepant corrections can propagate to disposition, an expensive risk if unresolved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of manufacturing steps documented before execution
rows.plain: Prevent workers from recording production steps before they actually happen. Records should show the true order of events and what was really done.
rows.imp_rat: Steps documented before execution violate contemporaneous recording, a core data-integrity principle; serious but sometimes procedural in impact.
rows.sat_rat: EBRs rarely prevent an operator from entering a step before physically executing it, so this remains a real gap. Timestamps are auto-captured at entry, so premature documentation is time-visible but not prevented in advance. It relies on operator discipline not to pre-fill fields ahead of doing the work, a known contemporaneous-recording weakness. Pre-documentation is a serious data-integrity finding that can invalidate records and trigger costly remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of test results with conflicting observed values across source records
rows.plain: Make sure the same test result has the same measured value in every original record. This prevents conflicting information from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting observed values across source records undermines a single trustworthy result; central to reliable compilation and serious for disposition.
rows.sat_rat: When data comes from multiple sources the EBR does not always reconcile them, so conflicts persist. Reconciling conflicting values across source records is slow and manual. Resolving conflicting observed values across systems demands analyst judgement. Conflicting source records trigger investigations with real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of data-integrity weaknesses left unidentified in controls for batch-record creation, modification, review, approval, and retention
rows.plain: Find as many weaknesses as possible in the safeguards for creating, changing, checking, approving, and keeping production records. This helps ensure the records remain accurate and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified data-integrity weaknesses across the record lifecycle threaten the trustworthiness of everything the EBR produces, with serious systemic consequences.
rows.sat_rat: ALCOA+ frameworks and inspection experience catch most weaknesses, yet subtle control gaps in modification and retention still slip through, leaving real recurring exposure. Structured GAMP-based checklists speed control review but a full data-integrity control walk-through still takes considerable time. Identifying gaps across create/modify/review/approve/retain demands seasoned data-integrity expertise that few reviewers fully possess. Mature audit templates keep incremental review cost modest once expertise is on hand.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed tests lacking a recorded result
rows.plain: Have fewer completed tests with no result written down. This ensures the test record shows what was found.
rows.imp_rat: Completed tests lacking a recorded result leave critical evidence gaps central to compilation and can block or corrupt disposition.
rows.sat_rat: EBR completeness checks reliably prevent tests from closing without a recorded result in most configurations. Completion checks run automatically so unresolved tests are flagged without manual scanning. System logic identifies tests lacking a result, reducing reliance on reviewer diligence. Missing results caught at attestation avoid costly late discovery in QA release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material-usage records containing an incorrect source-lot identifier
rows.plain: Have fewer material-use records that name the wrong production lot as the source. This helps track exactly which materials went into each product.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect source-lot identifiers break lot traceability central to recall capability with serious consequences if defective material must be traced.
rows.sat_rat: Scanned lot capture is reliable, but manually keyed lots remain a common error source. Lot capture by scan is instant; manual lot entry is slower and error-prone. Scanning removes the need for operators to transcribe complex lot numbers. Wrong source-lot breaks genealogy and can force recalls, a high-cost failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of test executions with required conditions omitted
rows.plain: Run fewer tests without all the required conditions in place or recorded. This helps make the test results meaningful and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Test executions with required conditions omitted leave the test context undocumented; central to valid results with serious impact on data defensibility.
rows.sat_rat: Templated condition fields help, but conditions not in the template are frequently omitted. Prompting for conditions at execution avoids later time reconstructing them. Knowing which conditions to capture still depends partly on analyst knowledge. Omitted conditions can invalidate a test and force repeats at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded test values containing digit, sign, or decimal-position errors
rows.plain: Have fewer test results with wrong digits, plus or minus signs, or decimal placement. This helps ensure decisions are based on the correct numbers.
rows.imp_rat: Digit/sign/decimal errors in test values are transcription defects central to accurate records; can flip a pass/fail decision with serious impact.
rows.sat_rat: Format masks and range checks catch gross errors but plausible in-range digit transpositions still slip through routinely. Digit/decimal checks add little time when built into field masks, but catching them manually is slow. Avoiding digit, sign and decimal-position slips is a classic manual-attention error that no amount of standard training fully eliminates. A misplaced decimal can force full batch investigation, a high-cost failure that occurs sporadically.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documented conditions are associated with the wrong test, sample, or batch
rows.plain: Lower the chance that recorded testing details are linked to the wrong test, sample, or batch. This helps keep results traceable and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Documented conditions associated with the wrong test/sample/batch corrupts traceability central to the record with serious misattribution consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Contextual linking helps but manual entry still permits conditions being tied to the wrong test or sample. Context-linked condition fields reduce time spent reattributing conditions. Guided association lowers the skill needed to tie conditions to the right test. Misassociated conditions can misdirect investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an out-of-limit process value as conforming before proceeding
rows.plain: Avoid treating a measurement outside the allowed range as acceptable. This helps ensure work does not continue when the process is not following instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an out-of-limit value as conforming lets the process proceed on failing data; central to conformance and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Built-in limit-checking on entered values makes accepting an out-of-limit value fairly reliably prevented where configured. Configured limit checks evaluate values instantly at entry, avoiding delayed detection. Automated range/limit validation removes reliance on the operator knowing every specification. Blocking out-of-limit acceptance prevents costly downstream nonconformance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completion attestations made while a required execution exception remains unresolved
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times workers say a step is complete while a required problem is still open. This helps ensure unfinished or unresolved work is not treated as done.
rows.imp_rat: Attestations made with a required exception unresolved falsely signal completion; central to attestation reliability with serious release risk.
rows.sat_rat: Where exception workflows are integrated, attestation over open exceptions is fairly reliably blocked. Exception status is linked to steps so attestation blocking is immediate where configured. The system can prevent signing over open exceptions, reducing operator judgement burden. Attesting over unresolved exceptions creates rework and disposition delays, mostly avoided by gating.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that permissions grant unauthorized access to regulated production functions or records
rows.plain: Prevent people from getting access to controlled production tasks or records unless they are allowed to do so. This helps protect the work and its required records.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized access to regulated functions/records is central to the MES's data-integrity and control role (high relevance), and severity is high given compliance breaches and potential falsified records.
rows.sat_rat: RBAC frameworks exist and are mature, but misconfiguration during setup still leaves recurring gaps that audits catch. Role-based access control setup is time-consuming to map against regulated functions during preparation. Correctly translating GMP segregation-of-duty rules into permission matrices demands scarce compliance-savvy administrators. Access reviews and validation cycles consume audit and IT labor budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required review approvals absent at readiness review
rows.plain: Make sure all required approvals are present when readiness is reviewed. This helps confirm that the batch is properly prepared to move forward.
rows.imp_rat: Absent required review approvals at readiness leave the record uncertified at a gate; central to the approval workflow with serious but recoverable impact.
rows.sat_rat: Routing and delegation features reduce absent-approval cases, but coverage gaps still leave approvals missing at review. Chasing absent reviewers at readiness review consumes time near the deadline. Required reviews depend on specific qualified approvers whose absence stalls the review. Missing approvals delay disposition, incurring holding and expediting costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of results linked to the wrong batch, sample, or test
rows.plain: Make sure test results are connected to the correct batch, sample, and test, so people do not make decisions using the wrong information.
rows.imp_rat: Results linked to the wrong batch/sample/test is a traceability defect that can cause wrong-batch release; central and serious.
rows.sat_rat: Structured linking cuts most mislinks, though similar sample/test identifiers still cause occasional errors. Linked entry at point of capture avoids downstream time spent untangling associations. Guided selection lowers the skill needed to attach results to the right sample or test. Wrongly linked results cause investigation and potential re-test costs when discovered.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a recorded result is associated with the wrong batch, sample, test, or execution
rows.plain: Make sure each recorded result is linked to the correct production run, sample, test, and activity. This prevents information from being assigned to the wrong product or task.
rows.imp_rat: Associating a result with the wrong batch/sample/test corrupts traceability central to the record with serious risk of misdisposition.
rows.sat_rat: Context-driven linking and scanning reduce wrong associations, but manual selection still allows mislinking under time pressure. Barcode/sample selection at entry is quick and avoids later reconciliation time. Dropdown and scanned associations reduce the analyst judgement needed to link results correctly. A mis-association can invalidate a batch decision, a high-cost but intermittent event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required execution or verification signatures missing from each batch record
rows.plain: Reduce the number of batch records missing signatures from people who were required to perform or check the work. Complete signatures show that the required steps were done and reviewed.
rows.imp_rat: Missing execution/verification signatures break attributability required for a compliant record; serious for accountability and inspection standing.
rows.sat_rat: EBR systems typically block progression until required execution/verification signatures are applied, making missing signatures much rarer than in paper. Electronic signature prompts capture signatures inline, saving the time of hunting missing sign-offs afterward. Enforced signature steps reduce dependence on reviewers to notice a missing signature. Missing signatures cause rework and delayed release, but electronic enforcement keeps these costs modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production runs with conflicting versions of the compiled batch record
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production runs that use different versions of the combined instructions and record for making a batch. Using one current version helps keep the work consistent and reviewable.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting versions of the compiled record create ambiguity over the true record of record; central to record integrity and serious for disposition and inspection.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic single-instance batch records make conflicting compiled versions uncommon compared to paper. Single-source-of-truth systems avoid time lost reconciling versions. System-controlled records reduce reliance on staff to identify the authoritative version. Version conflicts are costly if they reach review, but centralization keeps this rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of linking an exception flag to the wrong batch, unit, or manufacturing step
rows.plain: Make sure a warning about a problem is connected to the correct batch, item, and production step. This helps people investigate and fix the right issue.
rows.imp_rat: Linking an exception flag to the wrong batch/unit/step misdirects risk attention; central to accurate flagging and serious for release decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Context inheritance makes wrong-linkage of exception flags fairly uncommon where the flag is raised in-flow. Exceptions raised in-context inherit the current batch/unit/step immediately. Contextual capture reduces operator need to manually associate the flag correctly. Mislinked exception flags misdirect assessment and delay disposition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an analytical result is linked to the wrong batch or sample
rows.plain: Make sure each test result is connected to the correct batch and sample. This prevents the wrong result from being used to make a decision.
rows.imp_rat: Analytical result linked to the wrong batch/sample corrupts traceability central to accurate records with serious risk of misdisposition.
rows.sat_rat: Sample-to-result linking via scanning is fairly reliable but manual overrides leave residual risk. Scanning sample IDs at result entry keeps linkage fast and contemporaneous. Barcode association reduces the need for the analyst to manually judge which sample applies. An analytical result on the wrong sample can misdirect a batch decision, a costly rare failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of result entries missing required units, observations, or analyst attribution
rows.plain: Make sure every test result includes the required measurement units, observations, and the name or identity of the person who performed the test. This makes the results complete and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Result entries missing units, observations, or analyst attribution combine several integrity gaps central to defensible records with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Combined mandatory-field enforcement catches most missing elements, though free-text areas remain gaps. Required-field checks flag missing units, observations or attribution at entry. Mandatory fields reduce reliance on analyst memory for each element. Missing elements found late cause rework and review delay costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-record entries lacking traceability to the responsible person, material, equipment, or production event
rows.plain: Make sure every production-record entry shows who did the work, what materials and equipment were involved, and which production event it describes. This makes the record trustworthy and easier to check later.
rows.imp_rat: Entries lacking traceability to person/material/equipment/event break the attributability that defines a compliant record; central and serious.
rows.sat_rat: EBR audit trails reliably tie entries to person, equipment, and event, making traceability a mature strength. Automatic audit trails capture attribution without added time. System-captured metadata removes reliance on operators to manually record who/what/when. Strong traceability reduces investigation cost when questions arise.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dispatching a work order before required materials, equipment, or line clearances are ready
rows.plain: Send a production job to the floor only when the needed materials, equipment, and cleared work area are ready. This prevents delays and unsafe or incomplete starts.
rows.imp_rat: Dispatching a work order before materials/equipment/line clearances are ready undermines the MES's readiness-control role (relevance high) and is severe since premature dispatch can cause line clearance failures and cross-contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance and readiness gating is established but often relies on integrations to ERP/equipment that leave real coverage gaps. Automated readiness checks at dispatch save time otherwise lost to premature starts and line stoppages. System verification of material/equipment/clearance status reduces dependence on a coordinator's manual checklist. Preventing premature dispatch avoids wasted setup and clearance rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory obligations omitted from recommended batch-record requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the recommended production records include all rules that apply. This prevents important legal or safety requirements from being left out.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting applicable regulatory obligations from recommended requirements undermines compliant record structure, but it is a setup-stage error with detectable downstream consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Manual cross-referencing of overlapping regulations routinely leaves obligations omitted with no systematic check, so gaps recur unpredictably. Scanning the full landscape of applicable regulations takes weeks and there is no fast way to confirm completeness of coverage. Identifying every applicable obligation demands scarce senior regulatory expertise and deep domain knowledge that most teams lack in full. Comprehensive regulatory research consumes significant expert labor hours and external consulting spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of analytical data points recorded with an incorrect unit
rows.plain: Reduce the share of test results recorded with the wrong unit. Using the correct unit prevents the results from being misunderstood or used incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect units on analytical data can silently invert a pass/fail judgment; central to accurate results and serious in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Templated fixed units reliably prevent most unit mismatches, though free-entry fields still permit them. Pre-defined unit fields avoid time spent correcting unit errors later. Fixed-unit fields remove the analyst decision about which unit to record. Unit errors can distort specification comparisons and trigger costly review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded values paired with an incorrect measurement unit
