rows.plain: Reduce the number of recorded measurements that are labeled with the wrong unit, such as grams instead of kilograms. Correct units are needed to understand the values properly.
rows.imp_rat: Values paired with an incorrect measurement unit can misrepresent conformance centrally, with serious risk of wrong pass/fail decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Structured value/unit fields make incorrect pairings uncommon where templates are enforced. Locked unit labels eliminate rework time from mismatched units. Predefined units reduce dependence on the analyst pairing value and unit correctly. An incorrect unit pairing can cause a wrong disposition, a costly but infrequent failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of atypical results represented by conflicting records
rows.plain: Reduce the number of unusual results that are described differently in different records. Consistent records make it easier to understand what really happened.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting records for atypical results obscure the true failure signal; relevant to reliable evidence with serious consequences for investigation and release.
rows.sat_rat: Atypical results captured across systems are inconsistently reconciled, leaving conflicting records. Reconciling conflicting atypical-result records takes investigative time. Resolving conflicting atypical records demands experienced judgement. Conflicting records around atypical results are costly compliance risks.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed manufacturing steps left undocumented
rows.plain: Record every manufacturing step after it is completed. This provides a clear history of what was done.
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented completed manufacturing steps go to the heart of compiling a complete batch record; missing steps invalidate the record and can force rejection or reprocessing.
rows.sat_rat: Forced-flow EBRs with mandatory fields make an entirely undocumented completed step uncommon, giving mature coverage though not absolute. EBR systems enforce step-by-step navigation so operators cannot easily skip forward, but backlog under time pressure still leaves late-documented steps. Mandatory-field logic and sequential prompts reduce the vigilance needed to remember every step, offloading recall onto the system. Undocumented steps trigger costly investigations and batch holds, but electronic gating catches most before they compound.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of results recorded without a sample association
rows.plain: Make sure every test result is linked to the sample that was tested. This makes it clear which material each result belongs to.
rows.imp_rat: Results recorded without sample association break traceability central to a defensible record with serious risk of misattributed data.
rows.sat_rat: Required sample-association fields reliably block most unassociated results in well-configured EBRs. Mandatory sample linkage at entry prevents later time reattaching results. Required-association logic reduces the analyst effort to remember to link a sample. Results without a sample tie cause investigation costs when found.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of derived results disagreeing with their underlying measurements
rows.plain: Make sure calculated results agree with the measurements they came from, so people can trust the data.
rows.imp_rat: Derived results disagreeing with underlying measurements is a calculation-integrity defect central to accurate reporting with serious pass/fail impact.
rows.sat_rat: Validated in-system calculations reliably keep derived results consistent with underlying measurements. System-computed derived results are instantaneous and avoid manual recalculation time. Automated calculations remove the arithmetic skill burden from the analyst. Locked formulas prevent costly calculation errors that would otherwise require re-checking.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accessing a superseded work instruction for the active sterile fill-finish step
rows.plain: Make sure workers use the current work instructions for the active step of filling and finishing the product in a germ-free environment. This helps prevent outdated directions from causing mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Accessing a superseded work instruction for an active sterile step defeats the MES's job of enforcing correct procedures (high relevance) and is severe because it can produce out-of-spec or non-compliant sterile batches.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced document version control tied to the active step is a core, mature MES strength that rarely fails when deployed. MES serves the current effective work instruction instantly at the step, eliminating time spent locating the right revision. Version control removes the operator's burden of judging which instruction is current. Controlled document delivery avoids rework from wrong-revision execution at modest system cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an out-of-sequence manufacturing step as conforming
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of accepting a manufacturing step as correct when it was done in the wrong order. Following the right order helps ensure the product is made properly.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an out-of-sequence step as conforming masks a real process nonconformance central to conformance checking with serious release risk.
rows.sat_rat: Configured sequence logic and review-by-exception make accepting a nonconforming out-of-sequence step fairly reliable to catch. System sequence checks flag deviations instantly rather than at end-of-batch review. Automated sequence gating reduces reviewer judgement about whether order was acceptable. Wrongly accepted out-of-sequence steps can force batch disposition delays, but configured rules limit this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material-consumption events omitted from executed batch-step records
rows.plain: Fewer times that materials are used should be missing from the records for completed production steps. Complete records show what materials were actually used.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted material-consumption events leave gaps in material accountability central to the record; serious for reconciliation and traceability.
rows.sat_rat: Where scanning is enforced, omissions are fairly rare, but manual-entry steps still leave gaps. Material scanning at point of use is fast when integrated, but manual consumption logging can lag the actual event. Barcode-driven material entry reduces reliance on operator recall of what was consumed. Omitted consumption breaks reconciliation and yield accounting, causing costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that displayed throughput, yield, or OEE values misrepresent actual sterile fill-finish results
rows.plain: Make sure the displayed production numbers accurately show how much was made, how much met requirements, and how efficiently the work ran.
rows.imp_rat: Misrepresented throughput/yield/OEE for sterile fill-finish directly corrupts the measure-and-control CFJ (high relevance), and wrong metrics can drive bad production decisions or mask quality issues (high severity), though these are derived KPIs rather than raw source data. Capped at 7.5 mean per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Metric accuracy depends on correct source-tag mapping and calculation definitions, which commonly harbor silent errors, leaving this only acceptably served. Dashboards refresh throughput/yield/OEE in near real time, so latency is rarely the issue. Displayed metrics reduce the need for manual calculation but interpreting anomalies still requires analyst judgement. Misrepresented metrics drive poor decisions with real cost, and validating the calculation logic takes effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of executed steps lacking required evidence during the conformance check
rows.plain: Make sure every completed step has the required proof when checking whether instructions were followed. This shows what was actually done.
rows.imp_rat: Executed steps lacking required evidence at conformance check leave the record unverifiable at a critical gate with serious release-risk consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-field and completeness logic make executed steps lacking required evidence uncommon at the check. Review-by-exception surfaces missing evidence quickly rather than at a slow end-of-batch review. System completeness checks reduce reviewer expertise needed to spot missing evidence. Missing evidence at conformance drives batch holds, but electronic checks catch most before release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting instructions between the batch record and approved procedure
rows.plain: Reduce disagreements between the instructions in the production record and the approved procedure. This helps workers know exactly which instructions to follow.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting instructions between the batch record and approved procedure risk execution against the wrong instruction; central to control and serious in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Generating EBRs from the approved master makes internal conflicts uncommon where change control is disciplined. Controlled generation from the approved master reduces reconciliation time between record and procedure. Master-template sourcing removes reliance on operators reconciling record against SOP. Conflicting instructions cause execution errors and deviations, mostly avoided by controlled templates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of linking a completion attestation to the wrong batch or manufacturing step
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that a sign-off saying work is complete is attached to the wrong production batch or wrong manufacturing step. This keeps the records tied to the correct work.
rows.imp_rat: Linking a completion attestation to the wrong batch/step misattributes accountability central to traceable records with serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Session-locked step context makes wrong-step attestation linkage uncommon. Preset batch/step context means correct linkage costs no extra time. Contextual binding removes operator judgement about which step an attestation belongs to. Mislinked attestations cause traceability investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of analytical data points lacking required test, analyst, instrument, or source attribution
rows.plain: Reduce the number of test results that do not say which test, person, device, or original record they came from. This makes the results easier to trust and check later.
rows.imp_rat: Analytical data lacking test/analyst/instrument/source attribution weakens traceability central to defensible records; serious for integrity and inspection.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic capture of analyst, instrument and source is reliable when interfaced, but manually entered data often lacks full attribution. Auto-populated attribution from logins and instruments is faster than manual annotation. System-captured metadata reduces the analyst effort to attribute each data point. Missing attribution triggers documentation queries that consume review time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of executed batch steps with used equipment omitted
rows.plain: Record all equipment used during each completed production step. This provides a reliable history of what took part in making the product.
rows.imp_rat: Used equipment omitted from executed steps leaves traceability gaps central to the record; serious for investigations and cross-contamination tracing.
rows.sat_rat: Required-field enforcement makes omitted used-equipment fairly uncommon where configured. Prompted equipment fields save time but omissions still occur when steps are rushed. Mandatory equipment fields reduce reliance on operator memory to log every device. Omitted equipment breaks traceability, causing review delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of blank required process, material, or equipment entries in each batch record
rows.plain: Fill in every required detail about the steps, materials, and equipment used in each production record. Complete records make it possible to know exactly how the batch was made.
rows.imp_rat: Blank required entries leave the record incomplete, central to compilation completeness; consequences serious but often caught in review.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory field enforcement in EBR catches most blank process/material/equipment entries at point of execution, a mature strength. Inline mandatory-field enforcement avoids downstream time spent finding and filling blanks. System-required entries reduce reliance on operator vigilance to leave nothing blank. Blank entries drive rework loops, but required-field logic limits that expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured machine values differing from source readings during sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: The values recorded from the machine should match the values actually shown by the machine. This helps ensure that production records are accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Captured machine values differing from source readings undermines the measurement foundation of the MES (high relevance) and in sterile fill-finish can lead to undetected out-of-spec batches (high severity). Held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Captured-vs-source fidelity is good on integrated equipment but drift, buffering, and unit-scaling mismatches still cause recurring discrepancies. Direct machine integration captures values in real time, eliminating transcription lag. Automated data capture removes the operator skill needed for manual readings. Interface engineering carries cost but avoids the expense of manual data reconciliation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required supporting documents absent or unlinked in each batch record
rows.plain: Include every required supporting document in each production record and link it to the information it supports. This makes the record complete and easier to verify.
rows.imp_rat: Absent or unlinked supporting documents leave the record incomplete for defensibility; relevant to compilation completeness with serious inspection consequences.
rows.sat_rat: EBR attachment checklists help, but documents originating in other systems are frequently absent or unlinked. Hunting for unlinked supporting documents at review is time-consuming. Knowing the full required-attachment set relies on reviewer knowledge as much as system prompts. Missing supporting documents cause re-review cycles, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production exceptions unresolved when batch-record compilation is due
rows.plain: Resolve production problems before the deadline for compiling the production record. This helps keep the record on time and prevents open issues from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved production exceptions at compilation due-date create incomplete records; relevant to timely completion with moderate, mostly recoverable impact.
rows.sat_rat: Tracking systems show open exceptions but do not accelerate their resolution, leaving compilation exposed. Unresolved production exceptions at compilation due-date are a frequent schedule breaker. Exception closure depends on scarce SME and QA judgement, limiting throughput. Overdue exceptions drive delayed release and expediting costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a job under a quality hold or release restriction is advanced in the active queue
rows.plain: A job that is being held back for quality checks or approval should not be moved forward in the active work list. This helps prevent work from continuing before it is cleared.
rows.imp_rat: Advancing a job under quality hold overlaps the hold-enforcement job of the MES (high relevance) and carries severe consequences of releasing restricted product; slightly redundant with item 11 but still core.
rows.sat_rat: Release-restriction enforcement is mature and reliable, with residual risk mainly at MES-to-scheduling handoffs. Queue restrictions apply immediately once a quality hold is set. Automated queue gating reduces dependence on schedulers manually tracking release status. Blocking held jobs from advancing avoids waste of downstream resources on restricted work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attributing a completion attestation to someone other than the step executor
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that someone is recorded as confirming a completed step when another person actually performed it. This keeps records accurate and accountable.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing a completion attestation to a non-executor breaks attributability central to a compliant record with serious data-integrity consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced unique e-signature credentials make attributing attestation to a non-executor uncommon, barring credential sharing. Individual login for signing is quick and does not add meaningful time. Unique-credential e-signatures remove reliance on manual identity discipline. Misattributed attestations are integrity findings, strongly mitigated by unique logins.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of result entries lacking executor attribution at attestation
rows.plain: Make sure each result is linked to the person who carried out or confirmed the work. This creates a clear record of who was responsible.
rows.imp_rat: Result entries lacking executor attribution at attestation break accountability central to a compliant record with serious integrity consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced e-signature and user context make executor attribution highly reliable at attestation. Electronic signatures attach executor identity instantly at attestation. Login-based attribution removes any skill burden for identifying the executor. Reliable attribution avoids costly rework chasing unsigned entries.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded values lacking an available original observation for comparison
rows.plain: Make sure every recorded measurement can be compared with the original observation that produced it. This helps show that the recorded information is accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values without an available original observation break the audit trail central to data integrity; serious for verifiability at inspection.
rows.sat_rat: Unless raw data is captured into or linked from the EBR, recorded values frequently lack a readily available original for comparison. Retrieving the original observation for comparison can be slow when raw data sits in separate instrument systems. Knowing where to find and how to compare the source observation demands analyst familiarity. Absent traceable originals inflate investigation costs and can force re-testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of equipment-usage entries for equipment not actually used
rows.plain: Record equipment use only when the equipment was actually used. This keeps the records accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment-usage entries for unused equipment falsify the execution record; relevant to accuracy with serious investigation and traceability consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Nothing strongly prevents recording equipment not actually used, though it is a relatively infrequent event. Removing an unused-equipment entry is quick but relies on the operator noticing it. Preventing phantom equipment entries depends on operator attention rather than system logic. Phantom usage entries mislead investigations but rarely scrap product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of test executions with conflicting condition records
rows.plain: Have fewer tests whose recorded conditions disagree with each other. This makes it clearer what conditions the test actually used.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting condition records for test executions undermine a single defensible account; relevant to reliable records with serious result-validity impact.
rows.sat_rat: The EBR rarely cross-checks condition consistency, so conflicts persist unless caught in review. Detecting conflicting condition records is a slow manual review task. Reconciling condition conflicts requires reviewer expertise. Conflicting condition records prompt investigations that consume resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required data and metadata elements omitted from evaluation criteria for manufacturing and testing records
rows.plain: Leave out fewer required details about the data and how it was created when setting review rules for production and testing records. This helps keep records complete and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required data/metadata from evaluation criteria weakens completeness checks; relevant to structured evaluation with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Metadata elements are commonly overlooked because evaluation criteria are built ad hoc rather than from a validated master set. Defining complete data and metadata evaluation criteria adds considerable specification time. Metadata completeness demands data-integrity expertise that is unevenly available. Incomplete criteria lead to costly re-evaluations and audit findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a controlled production step proceeds with mandatory electronic batch record fields incomplete
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a required production step continues when important electronic records are unfinished. This helps ensure the needed information is recorded before work proceeds.
rows.imp_rat: Proceeding with incomplete mandatory eBR fields defeats the MES's record-integrity job (high relevance) and is severe because incomplete batch records can invalidate release and breach GMP.
rows.sat_rat: eBR mandatory-field enforcement is a mature, widely deployed control that reliably blocks incomplete records. Mandatory-field checks fire at record close without meaningful delay. Required-field enforcement removes reliance on operators remembering every mandatory entry. Complete-at-source records cut costly review-by-exception rework and batch-release delays.

rows.source_job: Connect shop-floor equipment to MES software
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of MES data points linked to incorrect PLC, sensor, or production-machine signals
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production readings connected to the wrong machine, sensor, or control signal. This helps ensure the production software shows accurate information.
rows.imp_rat: Data points linked to incorrect PLC/sensor/machine signals corrupt what the MES measures (relevant), and mislabeled signals can propagate errors into records (severity), though this is often a configuration-time issue caught in commissioning.
rows.sat_rat: Incorrect signal linkages are common because point-by-point verification is manual and rarely exhaustive. Mapping thousands of PLC/sensor signals to MES data points is a lengthy, tedious integration task. Correct signal-to-datapoint linkage needs controls engineers who understand both floor equipment and MES semantics. Integration labor is substantial though hardware cost is minimal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of checking an executed step against an obsolete instruction version
rows.plain: Make sure a completed step is checked against the current instructions, not an old version. This helps prevent mistakes caused by outdated guidance.
rows.imp_rat: Checking a step against an obsolete instruction version can pass a genuine nonconformance; central to correct conformance checking and serious in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: EBR version control makes execution against an obsolete instruction very uncommon. System serves the current effective version automatically, saving version-lookup time. Automatic version control removes reliance on operators knowing the current instruction revision. Checking against obsolete versions causes invalid conformance and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of observed production deviations without a corresponding nonconformance record
rows.plain: Make sure every observed production problem is officially recorded, so issues can be investigated and corrected.
rows.imp_rat: Deviations without a corresponding nonconformance record is a compliance-critical gap (severity) affecting the control mandate, but it is a downstream quality-management concern relative to core execution (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Only system-detected deviations reliably generate NCs; the many human-observed deviations frequently go unrecorded, making this poorly served. Automated NC creation from flagged deviations is fast, but detecting undocumented deviations still lags on manual observation. Capturing an observed deviation into an NC still depends heavily on staff recognizing and choosing to record it. Missing NC records drive costly audit findings and unmanaged quality risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a batch advancing past a required operation without completion
rows.plain: Prevent a batch from moving to the next step until the required operation is finished, so nothing is missed.
rows.imp_rat: A batch advancing past a required operation without completion breaks the MES's enforced sequencing (high relevance) and is severe since skipped steps yield non-conforming product.
rows.sat_rat: Sequential enforcement of operation completion is one of MES's most mature and dependable capabilities. Operation-completion gating checks are instantaneous and prevent time lost to rework from skipped steps. Enforced step sequencing removes the operator's burden of tracking completion manually. Preventing advance past incomplete operations avoids scrap and reprocessing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of process parameter values recorded inaccurately in the MES
rows.plain: Record the machine's operating information accurately in the electronic production record, so people can trust the records and make sound decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurately recorded process parameters weaken the control job (high relevance) and can invalidate batch release decisions (meaningful severity), but effects are somewhat parameter-specific rather than systemic.
rows.sat_rat: Automated tags are accurate but the residual manual-entry portion keeps parameter recording only acceptably reliable. Automated parameter capture is fast, but manually entered parameters still consume operator time. Where parameters are hand-keyed, accuracy still depends on operator care, only partly reduced by validation prompts. Inaccurate parameters trigger investigations and rework that outweigh capture-system costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed manufacturing steps attributed to the wrong batch
rows.plain: Make sure completed manufacturing steps are recorded under the correct batch. This prevents one batch's work from being mistakenly credited to another.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing completed steps to the wrong batch is a traceability defect central to compilation with serious consequences for batch integrity and traceability.
rows.sat_rat: EBRs bind entries to a locked batch record instance, so wrong-batch attribution is largely designed out. Batch context is selected once at login/session start, so no repeated time cost accrues per step. Barcode/batch-scan binding removes reliance on the operator correctly remembering which batch they are on. Misattributed steps can force scrap or re-review, but session-locked batch context prevents most cross-batch errors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attaching process data to the wrong manufacturing step
rows.plain: Make sure each piece of process information is linked to the correct manufacturing step. This prevents records from being attached to the wrong part of the work.
rows.imp_rat: Attaching process data to the wrong step is a traceability defect central to record accuracy; usually detectable but can mislead disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Step-scoped entry fields make attaching data to the wrong step uncommon, though multi-value screens still allow slips. Structured forms present the correct field at the current step, so little time is spent hunting where data belongs. Contextual field placement reduces the judgement needed to associate a value with its step. Misattached data causes review delays, but structured step context limits the frequency.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among applicable regulatory expectations for batch-record structure or retention
rows.plain: Resolve disagreements among the applicable rules about how batch records should be organized or kept. Clear decisions help ensure records are handled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among regulatory expectations for structure/retention affect compliance framing but are a moderate, resolvable setup concern.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts are frequently discovered late or left unresolved because no standard practice systematically surfaces and adjudicates them. Reconciling conflicting expectations across regimes is slow and iterative with repeated back-and-forth among experts. Resolving genuine regulatory conflicts requires rare interpretive judgement across multiple jurisdictions. Extended expert deliberation and legal review add substantial cost to each requirements recommendation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required result fields left blank at completion attestation
rows.plain: Leave fewer required result sections blank when confirming that the work is complete. This makes the final record complete and easier to trust.
rows.imp_rat: Required result fields blank at attestation leave evidence gaps central to a complete record; serious but typically caught before disposition.
rows.sat_rat: EBRs reliably prevent attestation while required fields remain blank, one of their strongest controls. Attestation-time completeness checks are instantaneous versus manual page review. Mandatory-field enforcement removes reliance on the signer noticing blanks. Blank required fields blocked at signing avoid costly post-release gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of duplicate result entries appearing to represent separate executions
rows.plain: Prevent the same result from being recorded more than once as if it came from separate tests. This keeps records accurate and avoids confusion about what was actually done.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate entries appearing as separate executions inflate the evidence record misleadingly; relevant to record accuracy with serious misinterpretation risk.
rows.sat_rat: Execution metadata helps separate repeats from duplicates, but ambiguity persists in some configurations. Distinguishing genuine repeats from duplicates can take reviewer time. Judging whether entries represent separate executions requires reviewer expertise. Misread duplicates can inflate or distort data at investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of equipment-usage entries containing an incorrect equipment identifier
rows.plain: Reduce the share of equipment-use records that name the wrong piece of equipment. This helps ensure the work history can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect equipment identifiers undermine equipment traceability relevant to record accuracy; serious for investigations and qualification linkage.
rows.sat_rat: Where equipment is scanned, incorrect identifiers are uncommon, but manual selection still allows picklist errors. Equipment scanning is fast; manual selection from lists adds minor time. Barcode/dropdown equipment selection reduces reliance on the operator recalling exact IDs. Wrong equipment IDs break traceability and can force investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validation deficiencies left unidentified across intended use, system boundaries, and lifecycle coverage
rows.plain: Find all important weaknesses in the checks, including how the system will be used, what it includes, and how it will be managed over time. Finding these gaps helps prevent compliance problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified validation deficiencies across use/boundaries/lifecycle threaten system trustworthiness; moderately relevant with resolvable-but-serious impact.
rows.sat_rat: No systematic method guarantees completeness, so lifecycle and boundary deficiencies routinely remain undiscovered until late. Discovering deficiencies across intended use, boundaries and lifecycle takes protracted iterative review. Spotting missing lifecycle coverage is heavily judgement-dependent and error-prone even for experts. Rework from late-found deficiencies drives significant unplanned cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic intervention entries attributed to the wrong individual
rows.plain: Reduce the number of sterile-handling records credited to the wrong person. Accurate records show who actually performed each action.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic intervention entries attributed to the wrong individual weaken accountability for contamination-relevant actions; relevant with serious integrity consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Session credentials make wrong-individual intervention attribution uncommon, though shared-gown scenarios add risk. Logged-in identity auto-populates intervention attribution instantly. Automatic user capture removes reliance on operators recording who intervened. Wrong attribution triggers investigation, largely prevented by session-bound identity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that production records subject to retention or hold obligations are irreversibly purged
rows.plain: Prevent production records that must be kept or protected from being permanently deleted. This ensures the records remain available when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Irreversible purging of records under retention/hold is infrequent (lower relevance to routine execution) but catastrophic when it happens, exposing the company to serious compliance and legal consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulated environments have retention controls, but purge routines occasionally reach records that should have been held, an irreversible failure. Retention holds are applied through policy engines that act promptly once triggered. Mapping which records fall under legal or regulatory hold demands compliance expertise that is not always available. Retention infrastructure exists but comprehensive coverage requires investment many sites limit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed manufacturing steps lacking a completion attestation
rows.plain: Make sure every completed production step has a recorded confirmation. This shows that the step was actually finished.
rows.imp_rat: Missing completion attestations break the attributability that a compliant batch record requires, though the underlying step may still be verifiable, tempering severity.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced e-signature checkpoints make a completed step lacking attestation rare where the EBR gates progression. Electronic signature prompts appear immediately at step close, so attestation happens contemporaneously rather than being deferred. The system blocks progression without a signature, so operators need no special discipline to attach attestations. Missing attestations are a top audit finding, but e-signature enforcement largely eliminates the rework they cause.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validation requirements lacking traceability to intended use, regulated risks, or governing obligations
rows.plain: Link every testing requirement to the product’s intended use, the risks it must control, or the rules it must follow. Clear links show why each test is needed.
rows.imp_rat: Validation requirements lacking traceability to use/risk/obligations is a system-lifecycle assurance gap; moderately relevant to record trust with resolvable impact.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices exist as standard practice but coverage is frequently incomplete, leaving untraced requirements that surface in audits. Building traceability matrices linking requirements to intended use and obligations is slow manual cross-referencing. Requires deep knowledge of regulations and validation practice to trace each requirement to its governing basis. Spreadsheet-based traceability is cheap in tools but expensive in analyst hours.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-record elements mapped to incorrect regulatory obligations
rows.plain: Make sure each part of the production record is linked to the correct legal requirement. This prevents records from being reviewed against the wrong rules.
rows.imp_rat: Elements mapped to incorrect regulatory obligations is a structuring error moderately relevant to compliant compilation with recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Manual traceability matrices are error-prone and misassignments slip through without automated validation. Mapping each record element to the correct obligation is a painstaking manual exercise with no shortcut. Correct mapping depends on expert interpretation of which clause governs which element, a high-skill task. Mapping errors trigger costly rework and re-review cycles when caught downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of machine signals associated with incorrect MES tags
rows.plain: Make sure machine readings are matched to the correct production labels, so records about what happened are accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Machine signals mapped to incorrect MES tags is a tagging-integrity defect central to accurate data capture (moderate relevance) with moderate severity since it typically surfaces during validation before impacting live batches.
rows.sat_rat: Mis-tagged signals persist frequently because validation of each tag against live data is inconsistent. Tag mapping and verification across machine signals is slow when done thoroughly. Correct tag assignment requires engineers fluent in both machine addressing and MES tag conventions. The effort is labor-intensive but not materially costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in whether the assigned manufacturing step satisfies its completion criteria
rows.plain: Make it clear whether the assigned production step really meets the conditions for being finished. This supports a confident and accurate sign-off.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in whether a step meets completion criteria affects attestation reliability; moderately central with resolvable-but-nontrivial consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Explicit criteria fields improve consistency, but subjective steps still leave interpretation to the operator. Embedded completion criteria display at the step, avoiding time spent consulting separate SOPs. On-screen acceptance criteria reduce the expertise needed to judge whether a step is truly complete. Ambiguous completion causes rework and deviations, and structured criteria reduce but do not eliminate it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of in-process batches with execution state lost or mismatched during replacement-system cutover
rows.plain: Reduce the number of production batches whose progress is lost or recorded incorrectly while operations are moved to replacement equipment. This helps prevent confusion and production errors during the changeover.
rows.imp_rat: Lost/mismatched batch state during replacement-system cutover is a one-time migration event (low routine relevance) but high severity, as in-process sterile batches could be scrapped or invalidated.
rows.sat_rat: Cutover of live in-process batches is one of the least reliable steps, with data model differences and timing gaps routinely causing lost or mismatched state despite best-effort reconciliation. Reconciling in-process batch execution state during cutover is time-consuming manual work that often extends the migration window. Correctly mapping and transferring live batch state between old and new systems demands deep expertise in both schemas, a rare and error-prone competency. State mismatches force batch holds, re-verification, or scrap, all of which consume significant labor and material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completion attestations associated with missing required process data
rows.plain: Only confirm that a step is complete when all required process information has been recorded. This helps ensure completion claims are supported by the necessary records.
rows.imp_rat: Attestations tied to missing process data misrepresent completeness; relevant to attestation reliability with serious but often detectable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Coupling attestation to required-data checks makes this failure mode uncommon where configured. Signature-gating tied to data completeness prevents attestation without slowing the operator materially. The system enforces data presence before signing, so operators need no special vigilance. Attestations over missing data are integrity findings, largely prevented by field enforcement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a completed MES backup is unusable for recovery because its content is corrupted
rows.plain: A finished copy of the production data should remain accurate and usable if it is needed to restore the system. Damaged or altered data could make recovery fail.
rows.imp_rat: An unusable corrupted backup is only relevant during disaster recovery (limited relevance to routine job) but severe when needed, potentially causing permanent loss of regulated production data.
rows.sat_rat: Backups complete routinely but their recoverability is frequently unverified, so unusable backups are a well-known lurking risk. Backups run on schedule automatically, so time is not the primary constraint. Judging backup usability requires periodic restore drills that skilled staff seldom perform routinely. Full restore-validation is costly, so many organizations skip it and discover corruption only during a real recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of documentation tasks with conflicting or unclear ownership across concurrent runs
rows.plain: Reduce the number of record-keeping tasks where it is unclear or disputed who is responsible while several runs happen at once. Clear responsibility helps records get completed without delays or omissions.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting/unclear documentation ownership across concurrent runs is a coordination issue; relevant to completeness with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Task ownership across overlapping runs is largely managed manually, leaving frequent conflicting or unclear assignments. Ownership confusion across concurrent runs causes delays as tasks bounce between staff. Clear allocation depends on supervisory coordination skill, not tooling. Duplicated or dropped tasks waste qualified staff effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved data discrepancies blocking batch-record compilation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of unresolved differences in the data that prevent the final production record from being completed. This helps the record be finished accurately and on time.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved data discrepancies blocking compilation is largely a workflow blocker; relevant to completing the record but consequences are internal delay.
rows.sat_rat: Discrepancy detection is partly automated, but the resolution loop remains manual and blocks progress unpredictably. Data discrepancies routinely stall compilation while investigations run, a chronic source of delay. Resolving discrepancies needs skilled cross-functional judgement that cannot be shortcut. Blocked compilation ties up staff and delays release, incurring real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of inspection and test results linked to the wrong batch, sample, or production step
rows.plain: Make sure inspection and test results are attached to the correct production batch, sample, and step. This keeps quality records accurate and traceable.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection/test results linked to the wrong batch or step corrupts quality decisioning (relevant) and could allow defective sterile product to be released (high severity), though the error is localized to quality-result linkage.
rows.sat_rat: Automated result linkage works well within MES but LIMS/instrument handoffs remain a recurring source of mislinked results. Context-driven linking of results to the active batch/sample is immediate at point of capture. System association reduces the technician's burden of manually mapping results to the right record. Correct linkage avoids costly mis-attribution investigations and re-testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production routings containing omitted required steps, materials, or resource assignments
rows.plain: Make sure each production plan includes every required step, material, and piece of equipment or staff support. Missing information can cause production to be incomplete or incorrect.
rows.imp_rat: Routings with omitted required steps or resource assignments affects execution correctness (relevant), with moderate severity since such omissions are often caught at routing validation before release.
rows.sat_rat: Routing templates and review checklists help, but omitted steps or resource assignments still slip through routinely. Building complete routings with all steps and resources is time-consuming during master-data setup. Routing authoring needs process engineers who know the full production sequence and BOM. Routing configuration is labor-heavy but a standard planned expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consumption records containing an incorrect lot identity for a consumed material or component
rows.plain: Make sure each record correctly identifies the batch of material or part that was used. This makes it possible to trace what went into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect lot identity in consumption records breaks material traceability (relevant to control) and in pharma can compromise recall scope and genealogy (notable severity), but it affects specific records rather than the whole execution engine.
rows.sat_rat: Scan-verified consumption is well established but manual overrides and unlabeled materials still let wrong-lot records through. Barcode/scan-based lot consumption is fast and avoids delays from manual lot lookup. Scan verification reduces reliance on operators reading and keying lot IDs correctly. Correct lot capture avoids costly traceability failures and recall scope expansion.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of equipment-usage entries missing required details
rows.plain: Make sure every record of equipment use includes all the required details. Complete records make it clear how and when the equipment was used.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment-usage entries missing required details weaken traceability relevant to record completeness with moderate-to-serious consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Required-field logic makes missing equipment details uncommon. Structured equipment forms make detail entry quick with defaults. Mandatory-field prompts reduce reliance on operator knowing which details are required. Missing equipment details cause review flags and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of manufacturing steps documented outside their execution sequence
rows.plain: Write down each manufacturing step in the order it was actually performed. This makes the work easier to follow and helps show that nothing was done out of sequence.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence documentation affects record chronology relevant to integrity, but the underlying data may remain valid, moderating severity.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced step ordering makes out-of-sequence documentation uncommon, though parallel operations still allow some. Sequential enforcement means little time is lost to out-of-order navigation where flow is locked. Forced sequencing removes the need for operators to track proper order themselves. Out-of-sequence documentation causes review flags, but flow control avoids most rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of units or lots whose genealogy records omit consumed materials, components, or production steps
rows.plain: Reduce the number of products or batches whose history leaves out materials used, parts added, or steps completed during production. This matters because people may be unable to trace how a product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Genealogy records omitting consumed materials or steps degrades traceability needed for recalls and audits (severity) and is fairly central to the measure/control job (relevance), yet remains a record-completeness issue rather than live control failure.
rows.sat_rat: Genealogy capture is a mature MES strength but omissions still occur at manual steps and unintegrated material sources. Genealogy is assembled automatically as material is consumed, saving time versus manual back-build. Automated as-built genealogy removes reliance on staff manually reconstructing the record. Complete genealogy avoids expensive recall-scope and traceability investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required production entries are unavailable when batch-record compilation begins
rows.plain: Make sure all required production records are available before the batch record is put together. This prevents delays and missing information.
rows.imp_rat: Required production entries unavailable when compilation begins causes gaps or delay; relevant to timely completion with moderate, recoverable impact.
rows.sat_rat: Integration gaps between execution and compilation systems mean required entries are sometimes unavailable when compilation starts. Waiting for upstream production entries before compilation can begin causes delay. Judging what data must be present relies on process knowledge more than automation. Late-arriving entries stall compilation, wasting scheduled effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restored MES records that are missing or corrupted
rows.plain: Make sure as many restored production records as possible are complete and unchanged. This helps people rely on the records after a recovery.
rows.imp_rat: Restored MES records that are missing or corrupted matters mainly in recovery scenarios (lower everyday relevance) but carries high severity when it occurs, since lost production data can breach retention obligations.
rows.sat_rat: Standard backup/restore tooling recovers most records intact, but partial corruption or missing records surface often enough to remain a real gap. Restore operations themselves are reasonably quick, but verifying record completeness afterward adds time. Confirming restored records are intact needs staff familiar with data structures and reconciliation. Restore testing consumes resources and downtime that many sites ration.

rows.source_job: Keep recording pace with the line
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a required process value being unavailable when its entry is due
rows.plain: Make sure each required process reading is available when it is time to record it. This keeps the records current with the work on the production line.
rows.imp_rat: A required process value unavailable when its entry is due causes gaps or delays; moderately relevant but often recoverable in workflow.
rows.sat_rat: The status quo cannot guarantee upstream data is ready when the entry is due, so gaps persist. When a value source (instrument, label) is delayed, the operator waits, and the EBR does nothing to accelerate availability. Knowing where to retrieve a required value still depends on operator familiarity with equipment and materials. Missing values stall the line and force placeholders, a recurring workflow cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of WIP records showing incorrect material identity, quantity, location, or production status
rows.plain: Keep work records accurate about which materials are being used, how much there is, where it is, and what stage of production it has reached. Accurate records help workers find and manage unfinished products.
rows.imp_rat: WIP records showing incorrect material identity, quantity, or status hurts real-time production visibility (relevant), with moderate severity since operators can often reconcile discrepancies before serious loss.
rows.sat_rat: WIP accuracy is generally good but unrecorded moves, splits, and manual adjustments leave recurring discrepancies. Transaction-driven WIP updates reflect status in near real time. System-tracked WIP reduces dependence on operators manually maintaining status and location. Accurate WIP avoids costly search, over-production, and inventory write-offs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of operator actions and step completions left unrecorded at MES workstations
rows.plain: Make sure workers’ actions and completed steps are recorded at the workstations. Complete records show what was done and when.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecorded operator actions and step completions creates gaps in the execution record (relevant), with moderate severity because missing entries can trigger review holds but rarely cause irreversible product harm.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked step-by-step enforcement in modern MES makes skipping most steps difficult, but manual bypasses, offline moments, and non-enforced actions still leave a meaningful fraction unrecorded. Electronic work instructions and guided screens capture step completions in real time, so unrecorded actions rarely stem from slowness. Enforced electronic signoffs reduce the judgement operators need to record correctly, but recording still relies on operator diligence to actually click through each step. Gaps in the execution record cost rework and audit effort, and the workstation logging itself adds little marginal cost once deployed.

rows.source_job: Synchronize production data with connected systems
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of confirmed completions, consumptions, and quality results not received by connected enterprise systems
rows.plain: Make sure connected company computer systems receive records of completed work, used materials, and quality results. This keeps information consistent across the business.
rows.imp_rat: Confirmed completions/consumptions not received by enterprise systems breaks integration integrity (relevant to end-to-end control) with moderate severity since discrepancies are usually detectable and reconcilable.
rows.sat_rat: Mature MES-to-ERP integration with acknowledgement handling and retry queues catches the large majority of confirmations, yet edge-case failures and silent drops still leak into enterprise systems, leaving real recurring gaps. Standard messaging middleware and store-and-forward queues typically deliver confirmations to ERP within seconds, so timing is rarely the felt loss when this need fails. Once integration mappings are configured by specialists, day-to-day operators need no skill for transactions to flow, though troubleshooting a stuck interface still demands scarce IT/integration expertise. Missed transactions cause costly inventory and financial reconciliation work, but standard error-queue reprocessing keeps most of that cost contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required batch-record approvals outstanding at the compilation deadline
rows.plain: Have fewer required batch records still waiting for approval when the records must be compiled. This helps the work stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Outstanding approvals at the deadline are a timeliness/delay issue relevant to throughput but rarely cause irreversible harm since the approval still occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow routing and dashboards exist, but they surface the backlog rather than eliminate it, leaving predictable last-minute pileups. Approval bottlenecks near deadlines are a chronic pain, with reviewers and QA often the rate-limiting resource at compilation cutoff. Approval requires qualified reviewers whose scarcity drives the delays, so the burden falls on limited skilled staff. Outstanding approvals at deadline drive overtime and expedited-review costs and delay product release revenue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that automatic failover does not activate when the active MES instance becomes unavailable
rows.plain: Make sure the backup computer system takes over automatically if the main production system stops working. This helps prevent operations from stopping unexpectedly.
rows.imp_rat: Failover not activating on instance failure is an availability-continuity concern (peripheral to the measure/control job itself) but severe when it strikes, causing production-line downtime for critical activities.
rows.sat_rat: Mature HA/failover technology works well when tested, though untested failover paths occasionally fail to activate. Configuring and testing failover clustering is a defined but non-trivial preparation activity. High-availability setup requires infrastructure specialists familiar with clustering and load balancing. Redundant nodes and HA licensing add meaningful cost that is usually budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured run, idle, or fault states differing from the actual machine state
rows.plain: The recorded machine status should match what the machine is really doing, whether it is running, waiting, or having a problem. This helps people and control systems respond to the true situation.
rows.imp_rat: Captured run/idle/fault states differing from actual machine state affects performance monitoring accuracy (moderate relevance) and moderate severity, as state mislabeling mainly distorts OEE rather than product quality directly.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated state capture is reliable on connected assets, but ambiguous micro-stops and unmapped fault codes still cause state mismatches. Machine-state capture via direct integration is real time with no reporting lag. Automated state detection removes reliance on operators manually classifying run/idle/fault. Accurate state capture underpins OEE-driven cost decisions and interface cost is modest once built.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the underlying cause of an MES fault
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the basic cause of a computer system problem will be mistaken. Finding the real cause helps prevent the wrong fix and further problems.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the underlying cause of an MES fault is fairly central to controlling production since it drives corrective action, and severity is moderate as wrong diagnosis prolongs downtime, but the ceiling caps this at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Logs and diagnostics exist but are fragmented, so misattribution of fault causes remains common. Root-cause analysis of MES faults can drag on for days while production waits. Correctly diagnosing the underlying cause depends heavily on scarce senior engineers' experience and intuition. Prolonged investigations and misdiagnoses waste labor and downtime resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production records corrupted, duplicated, or lost after interface data flow resumes
rows.plain: Prevent production information from being damaged, repeated, or lost when a repaired system connection starts moving data again. This keeps the production record complete and accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Records corrupted/duplicated/lost after interface resumes is an edge-case recovery condition (low relevance) with moderate-to-high severity depending on reconciliation ability.
rows.sat_rat: Buffering helps, but data-flow interruptions routinely produce duplicated, lost or corrupted records that require painful cleanup. Reconciliation after interface resumption is time-consuming and often manual. Detecting duplicated or lost records across interfaces requires specialized integration knowledge. Message queuing and reconciliation tooling add cost that is unevenly deployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed tests represented by duplicate result entries
rows.plain: Make sure each completed test is recorded only once. This prevents duplicate results from making the records inaccurate or confusing.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate result entries for completed tests are rework/ambiguity issues; relevant to clean records with moderate risk of misinterpretation.
rows.sat_rat: Test-bound result fields make duplicates uncommon in the EBR, though repeats can still create apparent doubles. One-result-per-test structures avoid time reconciling duplicate entries. Constrained result slots reduce the need for analysts to police duplicates. Duplicate result entries can cause miscounts and costly clarification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incomplete batch record is routed for review
rows.plain: The supervisor should not send a batch record for review until all required information is included. A complete record lets reviewers understand what was done and confirm it was acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Routing an incomplete record for review is rework/inefficiency that is caught by reviewers; relevant to workflow but consequences are largely absorbed internally.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch records with required-field enforcement catch many incompleteness cases before routing, though attachments and free-text sections still slip through. Completeness checks at routing add some upfront time but prevent slower rework cycles later. EBR systems increasingly enforce mandatory fields, reducing reliance on a reviewer catching every blank manually. Routing incomplete records wastes reviewer time and rework, but electronic completeness gates blunt most of that cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in issuing the readiness attestation beyond the disposition-review cutoff
rows.plain: Issue the confirmation that a batch is ready without waiting too long after the review deadline. This helps the work move forward on time.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in issuing the readiness attestation past a review cutoff is a timeliness issue; relevant to throughput with largely recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow deadlines and reminders exist but do not reliably prevent cutoff overruns. Attestation delays beyond the disposition cutoff are a recurring, directly time-defined failure. Attestation timing hinges on availability of the qualified attesting person, a scarce-resource constraint. Late attestation delays release and can breach supply commitments, costing revenue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical deviations, quality holds, or interventions omitted from shift handover
rows.plain: Make sure the outgoing shift tells the incoming shift about all important problems, pauses, and special actions. This helps the next shift continue the work safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical deviations or quality holds from shift handover can propagate defects across shifts (moderate severity), but it is a handover-process error somewhat peripheral to the MES's core measure-and-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Shift handover is still largely verbal or free-text logbook practice weakly integrated with MES deviations, so critical holds and interventions are routinely lost across the shift boundary. Handover happens under end-of-shift time pressure, so critical items are often rushed rather than delayed per se. Effective handover depends heavily on an experienced outgoing operator knowing what matters and communicating it, making outcomes highly skill- and vigilance-dependent. Omitted deviations lead to expensive downstream quality escapes and duplicated interventions, and MES rarely structures handover to prevent this.

rows.source_job: Keep recording pace with the line
rows.stmt: Reduce the time between completion of line activity and completion of its batch-record entry
rows.plain: Reduce the delay between finishing a task on the production line and writing it down in the lot’s records. This keeps the records up to date with what is happening on the line.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between line activity and record entry challenges contemporaneity but the entry still occurs; moderately relevant with modest consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-timestamps expose latency but do not close it when workload spikes. Contemporaneous prompting shortens the gap, but manual entry during busy operations still forces after-the-fact catch-up. Timely entry depends on the operator balancing recording against physical tasks, a persistent attention burden. Delayed entries risk data-integrity findings and rework that carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of analytical data points duplicated in the batch record
rows.plain: Reduce the number of test results that are entered more than once in the production record. This saves time and lowers the chance of recording errors.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicated analytical data points are largely rework/clutter caught in review; relevant to clean compilation but low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Structured single-entry fields limit duplication, though multi-system compilation can still introduce duplicates. Duplicate checks run in the background rather than requiring manual scanning. System detection reduces reliance on the analyst spotting duplicates. Duplicated data points cause confusion and rework when reconciled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a completed batch awaits required final testing results before disposition
rows.plain: Reduce the time a finished production lot waits for final test results before someone decides whether it can be released. This helps avoid unnecessary delays in completing the work.
rows.imp_rat: Time a batch awaits final testing before disposition is a delay/throughput factor; relevant to timeliness with largely recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Automated result posting reduces missed-result surprises, but does not change the fundamental testing lead time driving the wait. The core loss here is delay, and EBR-LIMS integration can shorten it, but batches routinely still wait on external lab turnaround the system cannot accelerate. Little specialized skill is involved; the wait is procedural and testing-throughput bound rather than expertise-limited. Idle batches awaiting results tie up working capital and warehouse space, a cost the status quo only partly mitigates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a compilation issue remains unassigned because ownership is unclear
rows.plain: Make sure someone is clearly responsible for fixing a problem when documents or information are being put together. This helps prevent the problem from being left unresolved.
rows.imp_rat: An unassigned compilation issue from unclear ownership is a workflow/coordination gap; relevant to closure but consequences are largely internal delay.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow assignment rules help, but ownership gaps still let some compilation issues sit unassigned. Unassigned issues sit idle until someone notices, adding delay. Assigning ownership needs coordination judgement rather than system logic. Idle unassigned issues delay release, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of MES transactions failing to reach the intended enterprise system
rows.imp_rat: MES transactions failing to reach enterprise systems directly undermines the integration that supports production control (fairly central), with moderate severity since data gaps can often be reconciled.
rows.sat_rat: Guaranteed-delivery messaging and retry queues are mature, but misconfigured transactions still occasionally fail to reach targets. Building and testing enterprise integration interfaces takes considerable preparation time. Integration middleware and message-queue expertise is required and moderately available. Middleware licensing and integration effort carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of unauthorized MES configuration changes left uncorrected
rows.imp_rat: Uncorrected unauthorized MES configuration changes can degrade control integrity and compliance (notable severity), but this is a governance error of moderate centrality to the core production-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and audit trails catch many unauthorized changes, but some remain uncorrected between reviews. Detecting and reverting unauthorized changes can lag well behind when they occur. Distinguishing unauthorized from legitimate changes requires knowledgeable review of change logs. Continuous configuration monitoring adds tooling and staffing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a known MES vulnerability remains unpatched after a relevant security fix becomes available
rows.imp_rat: Leaving a known MES vulnerability unpatched exposes production systems to compromise (notable severity), yet security patching is a supporting maintenance activity of moderate centrality to the measure-and-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Validation caution keeps patches from breaking production, but leaves known vulnerabilities exposed far longer than in standard IT. Patch cycles for validated MES environments are slow because every change must be re-validated before deployment. Assessing patch relevance and validation impact requires specialized security and MES knowledge. Re-validation for each patch is costly, incentivizing deferral.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying a correction to the wrong batch, manufacturing step, or field
rows.plain: Make sure a corrected entry is applied to the right batch, manufacturing step, and information field. A correction in the wrong place could make the records misleading.
rows.imp_rat: Applying a correction to the wrong batch/step/field corrupts the wrong record (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked lower.
rows.sat_rat: Field-scoped correction dialogs make wrong-target corrections fairly uncommon. Selecting the field to correct is quick within the record context. In-context correction reduces the operator's chance of picking the wrong target, though care is still needed. Misapplied corrections corrupt the wrong record, causing rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of corrections lacking the original entry, revised entry, reason, attribution, or sequence
rows.plain: When an entry is corrected, keep the original, the new entry, the reason for the change, who made it, and the order of events. This makes the records understandable and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Corrections lacking original/revised/reason/attribution violate audit-trail rules (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Audit-trail-enforced correction fields make incomplete correction metadata uncommon. Structured correction dialogs prompt all required elements quickly. Mandatory reason/attribution fields remove reliance on operators knowing what to capture. Corrections missing elements are integrity findings, largely prevented by enforced fields.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a batch decision relying on an erroneous entry before correction is complete
rows.plain: Prevent a wrong entry from being used in deciding what to do with a batch while the mistake is still being fixed. This helps keep the decision based on correct information.
rows.imp_rat: A batch decision relying on an erroneous pre-correction entry risks a bad disposition (severity), but capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Little in the status quo prevents an interim decision on an uncorrected value, so risk persists. Corrections are timestamped, but a decision can still be made before the correction posts. Avoiding reliance on erroneous data depends on reviewer awareness of pending corrections. Decisions on erroneous entries can misdispose batches, a high-cost error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that completion is attested while assigned tests remain unfinished
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that someone says all required tests are finished when some are still incomplete. Accurate completion records prevent unfinished work from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Attesting completion while tests remain unfinished falsifies status (severity), but ranking holds it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: EBR completeness gating reliably prevents attesting while tracked tests remain unfinished. Completion checks at attestation are instant versus manual verification. System enforcement reduces reliance on the signer verifying all tests are done. Blocking premature attestation avoids costly incomplete releases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exceptional test outcomes lacking required documentation at attestation
rows.plain: Fewer unusual test results should be missing the required written explanation when someone confirms the results are complete. This helps create a clear record of what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Exceptional test outcomes lacking documentation at attestation affect record completeness, but as a lower-ranked defect it stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Completeness checks rarely enforce exceptional-outcome documentation specifically, so gaps remain. Attestation checks flag missing documentation quickly when configured. Recognizing exceptional outcomes needing documentation depends on analyst judgement. Undocumented exceptional outcomes are serious compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of test dispositions conflicting with analytical values or acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Reduce the number of test decisions that disagree with the measured results or the rules for passing and failing. This helps ensure test results lead to correct decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Dispositions conflicting with analytical values or criteria risk wrong release decisions (severity), held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where limit checks are configured conflicts are flagged reliably, but manual dispositions still allow inconsistencies. Automated spec comparison flags conflicts faster than manual checking. System acceptance-criteria checks reduce reliance on analyst judgement. Disposition conflicting with data is a high-cost compliance failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a correction is applied to the wrong batch, sample, test, or result entry
rows.plain: Make sure a correction is made to the right batch, sample, test, or result. This prevents one item's information from being changed by mistake.
rows.imp_rat: A correction applied to the wrong batch/sample/test corrupts the wrong record (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked lower.
rows.sat_rat: Entry-specific correction interfaces reduce but do not eliminate misapplied corrections. Context-targeted corrections avoid time fixing the wrong entry later. Selecting the correct entry to fix still requires analyst attention. Corrections applied to the wrong entry compound errors at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dependent calculations, assessments, or batch-status fields retaining the erroneous value after correction
rows.plain: After a mistake is corrected, fewer related calculations, evaluations, or product-status records should still show the old wrong value. This helps keep later decisions and records accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Dependent fields retaining erroneous values after correction propagates error (severity), but held at 4.0 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Linked calculations often recompute, but disconnected assessments and status fields can retain stale values. Automatic recalculation of dependents after correction saves manual re-work time. Cascading updates reduce reliance on analyst re-deriving dependents. Stale dependent values can misdirect disposition at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a corrected entry is completed without required review
rows.plain: Make sure every corrected test record is completed and reviewed as required. This helps keep test information trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: A corrected entry completed without required review is a control gap of real severity, but held at 4.0 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Correction-review workflows are a fairly mature control, reliably requiring review where configured. Review workflows route corrections quickly but add a controlled step. Workflow gating reduces reliance on analysts remembering to seek review. Enforced review prevents costly unreviewed corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a documented atypical result is unavailable during batch review
rows.plain: Make sure a recorded unusual test result is available when the batch is reviewed. This helps reviewers make decisions using all relevant information.
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable atypical result during batch review is relevant to compilation completeness, but under the batch-2 ceiling and as a review-stage gap it is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: If captured in the EBR it is reliably retrievable, but atypical data often lives in separate systems and can be missing at review. Electronic retrieval is fast once documented, but scattered raw data slows access. Locating atypical documentation at review may require system familiarity. Unavailable atypical documentation at review delays release at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of results recorded against the wrong sequence step
rows.plain: Record fewer results under the wrong step in the testing process. Correctly matched results make it clear what was tested and when.
rows.imp_rat: Results recorded against the wrong sequence step misrepresent execution order (moderate severity/relevance), held at the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced execution reduces misplacement, but off-sequence or override actions still cause misrecording. Guided sequencing keeps entry aligned to the current step without delay. Step-driven workflows reduce reliance on analyst tracking of position. Results against the wrong step cause rework and confusion at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of results attributed to samples from the wrong batch
rows.plain: Make sure each test result is linked to the correct production batch. Otherwise, one batch could wrongly receive credit or blame for another batch’s result.
rows.imp_rat: Results attributed to the wrong batch is a serious data-integrity error, but the ranking constraint holds it at the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Context-driven batch linkage is fairly reliable but manual selection leaves residual wrong-batch risk. Batch linkage at entry avoids downstream time reattributing samples. Guided batch selection reduces analyst judgement burden. Wrong-batch attribution is a high-cost failure if it reaches disposition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of results associated with the wrong sampling location or collection point
rows.plain: Make sure each test result is linked to the correct place where the sample was collected. Otherwise, a clean or contaminated result could be assigned to the wrong location.
rows.imp_rat: Results tied to wrong sampling location misattribute data (moderate severity), but under the batch-2 cap it stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Location data is often entered manually and not tightly validated, so misattribution recurs. Capturing sampling location at collection avoids later reconstruction time. Correctly recording sampling point depends on analyst knowledge of the plan. Wrong location attribution can invalidate results at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sample identifiers assigned to different batch samples
rows.plain: Assign fewer sample labels to more than one batch sample. Each sample should have its own clear identity so results cannot be mixed up.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate sample identifiers across batch samples threaten traceability (moderate severity and relevance), held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where the system assigns unique IDs duplicates are rare, but manual assignment can still collide. System-generated unique IDs avoid time resolving duplicate assignments. Automated ID assignment removes analyst effort and error. Duplicate identifiers can cause mixups with costly consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch-record content becomes corrupted or inaccessible before review
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that production records become damaged or cannot be opened before someone checks them. Reviewers need to be able to see the full record.
rows.imp_rat: Record content corrupted or inaccessible before review threatens the record itself (severity), but ranking caps it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated, backed-up EBR systems make record corruption or inaccessibility rare — a strong status-quo point. Backups and redundancy mean little time is lost to corruption events. Validated infrastructure removes reliance on staff to safeguard record integrity. Robust storage avoids costly data-loss recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-record fields requiring correction during compilation
