rows.plain: Reduce the number of mistakes that must be fixed in production records before they can be completed. Fewer corrections make record completion faster and more reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Fields requiring correction during compilation is routine rework absorbed in the workflow; relevant to efficiency but low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Field validation catches many errors at entry, but fields still frequently require correction during compilation. Corrections during compilation add rework time, though inline validation reduces their number. Point-of-entry validation reduces reliance on downstream reviewers to catch field errors. Each correction consumes reviewer effort, a recurring moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production runs with required source records absent from the inspection package
rows.plain: Make sure the inspection file includes all required original records for each production run. This allows others to review what happened and confirm the work was done properly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing source records from the inspection package hurts inspection-readiness (severity), but it ranks lower here and stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Linkage improves inclusion, but cross-system source records are still sometimes absent from the package. Assembling inspection packages with all source records is time-consuming when records span systems. Knowing the complete source-record set relies on experienced reviewers. Missing source records in inspection risk findings and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a nonconforming batch-record management approach is judged conforming
rows.plain: Make sure a way of managing batch records that does not follow required rules is not mistakenly accepted as compliant. This helps keep records legally and properly managed.
rows.imp_rat: Judging a nonconforming approach as conforming carries real compliance severity, but per ranking it is capped at 4.0 in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: False acceptance of nonconforming approaches is a well-known chronic failure mode that current subjective assessment poorly guards against. Reaching a defensible conformity judgement takes extended deliberation. Distinguishing true conformance requires senior regulatory judgement that is scarce. A false-positive conformance can be enormously costly in later remediation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of modified batch-record entries whose prior values cannot be reconstructed
rows.plain: Keep as few changed production records as possible when the earlier values can no longer be recovered. This matters because people need to see what was originally recorded and what was changed.
rows.imp_rat: Modified entries whose prior values cannot be reconstructed breaks audit-trail integrity (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporary EBR systems reliably retain prior values through audit trails, so this is well served where such systems are deployed. Modern audit-trail features make reconstructing prior values fast. Standard EBR audit trails reduce reliance on specialist effort to recover values. Built-in audit-trail capability makes this low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record changes lacking a documented reason
rows.plain: Every change to the production record should include a written reason. This makes it possible to understand and review what was changed and why.
rows.imp_rat: Changes lacking a documented reason violate data-integrity expectations (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory reason-for-change is a mature EBR control, though free-text reasons can still be vague. Reason-for-change prompts capture rationale in real time with no delay. Enforced reason fields reduce dependence on user discipline. Feature is standard and adds negligible cost.

rows.source_job: Import master data into MES software
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of imported source values assigned to incorrect MES fields or codes
rows.imp_rat: Assigning imported source values to incorrect MES fields corrupts data used for control, giving moderate relevance, but such mapping errors are usually detectable and correctable, bounding severity.
rows.sat_rat: Import mapping errors are common because full reconciliation of every field and code is rarely completed. Field-by-field data mapping and code translation is a slow, detailed preparation task. Correct mapping needs analysts who understand both source and MES data models. Mapping and reconciliation labor is significant but not material-heavy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required interventions, stoppages, rejects, and batch events omitted from production reports
rows.imp_rat: Omitting interventions, stoppages, and batch events from production reports weakens visibility into production, a moderately central concern, though the underlying control still functions and consequences are moderate.
rows.sat_rat: MES reports faithfully reflect whatever was captured, so their completeness is only as good as event capture — interventions and rejects logged out-of-band still get omitted at a moderate rate. Automated report generation pulls logged events on demand, so production reports are produced quickly once data exists. Reports auto-compile without analyst effort, but completeness depends on operators having correctly recorded events upstream. Missing events distort yield and OEE accounting and drive misinformed decisions, though the reporting engine itself is cheap to run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that corruption in stored MES backups remains undetected after integrity verification
rows.imp_rat: Undetected corruption in MES backups is severe when a restore is needed since data may be unrecoverable, but backup integrity is a peripheral recovery concern rather than central to controlling live production.
rows.sat_rat: Basic checksums catch gross corruption, yet subtle undetected corruption in stored backups remains a recognized blind spot. Integrity checks run in the background without much added wait. Interpreting integrity-check results and configuring meaningful verification requires backup-domain expertise. Checksum verification is cheap to run but thorough deep verification is often skipped to save resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production-impacting interface deviations remaining undetected after integration validation
rows.imp_rat: Undetected interface deviations after integration validation can silently disrupt production data flow, but this is a validation-completeness error of moderate centrality and consequence to the core control job.
rows.sat_rat: Validation catches many deviations, but production-impacting ones still escape into live operation with notable frequency. Integration validation cycles are lengthy and often compressed under schedule pressure. Designing test cases that expose production-impacting interface deviations needs deep integration knowledge. Building realistic integration test coverage is expensive, so gaps persist.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of classifying an incompatible ERP, SCADA, or control-system interface as compatible
rows.imp_rat: Classifying an incompatible ERP/SCADA interface as compatible risks integration failure, but it is a validation-judgment error caught before go-live, giving moderate relevance and recoverable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility is often asserted rather than tested at acquisition, so incompatible interfaces are frequently misclassified as compatible until deployment. Interface compatibility checks stretch acquisition timelines because they depend on vendor responses and integration specialists' availability. Judging true ERP/SCADA/control-system compatibility from datasheets requires rare integration expertise that is easily overestimated during selection. Paper compatibility claims cost little to accept but drive large integration overruns when wrong.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile-product exposure while required batch entries are completed
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged sterile-product exposure while entries complete has contamination severity, but it is peripheral to the record job and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Nothing in typical EBR practice shortens exposure during in-isolator recording, leaving this largely unaddressed. Time completing entries prolongs sterile-product exposure, and manual EBR entry adds to that duration. Minimizing exposure depends on operator dexterity managing gloves and interface simultaneously. Extended exposure raises contamination risk and potential scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of interruptions to critical aseptic manipulations caused by recording demands
rows.imp_rat: Interruptions to aseptic manipulations by recording demands carry contamination severity, but as peripheral to record compilation it is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: The status quo forces a trade-off between recording and manipulation, poorly mitigated today. Recording demands interrupt critical aseptic manipulations because entry cannot be deferred safely. Managing the interruption relies on operator judgement about when to pause manipulation. Interruptions to aseptic work raise contamination risk and potential batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recording equipment usage when the equipment status is unacceptable for the batch step
rows.imp_rat: Recording equipment usage when status is unacceptable has quality severity, but is a lower-ranked defect held at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Only some EBRs enforce equipment-status checks, so this remains partially controlled. Status checks against equipment (calibration, clean) can be instant where integrated. System status gating reduces reliance on operators verifying equipment acceptability. Using unacceptable-status equipment can invalidate a batch, a high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material-usage records attributed to a batch step where consumption did not occur
rows.imp_rat: Material-usage records at steps where consumption did not occur are false entries (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Step-scoped material entry limits this, but nothing fully prevents mis-stepped consumption records. Correcting a misattributed consumption record is quick but relies on noticing it. Preventing consumption records on non-consuming steps depends on operator attention. False consumption records corrupt reconciliation, causing investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of recorded step-sequence changes without corresponding line activity
rows.imp_rat: Recorded sequence changes without corresponding line activity signal integrity concerns (severity), but ranked lower here.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails expose sequence changes but do not confirm corresponding line activity occurred. Recording a sequence change is quick but verifying it matches the line takes attention. Preventing unwarranted sequence changes depends on operator and reviewer judgement. Phantom sequence changes confuse review and cause rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a corrected value conflicting with contemporaneous manufacturing evidence
rows.imp_rat: A corrected value conflicting with contemporaneous evidence signals inconsistency (severity), kept below ceiling as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Little cross-checks a corrected value against other records, leaving this weakly controlled. Checking a corrected value against other evidence takes reviewer time not aided by the tool. Ensuring consistency with contemporaneous evidence relies on reviewer expertise. A corrected value conflicting with evidence is a serious integrity concern with high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of valid entries unintentionally altered during correction
rows.imp_rat: Valid entries unintentionally altered during correction damage good data (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Field-scoped edits make unintended alteration of adjacent valid entries uncommon. Correcting one field without touching others is fast in field-level edit modes. Field-level correction reduces reliance on operator care not to disturb valid entries. Altering valid entries during correction causes avoidable rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between an aseptic intervention and completion of its documentation
rows.imp_rat: Delay between aseptic intervention and its documentation risks data-integrity gaps (severity), but timing lag is peripheral to core compilation and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Deferred entry raises the chance of forgotten or inaccurate detail, weakly controlled today. Aseptic priorities force intervention documentation to be deferred until manipulations end, extending the delay. Timing depends on operator judgement to balance sterile work against recording. Delayed intervention documentation risks integrity findings and investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between flagged exception details and the observed line condition
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between flagged exception and observed line condition undermines record accuracy (severity moderate), but it is a narrower exception-detail issue kept below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured fields help, but free-text condition descriptions still diverge from observation. Flagging in real time is possible, but detail accuracy suffers when done after the fact. Matching flagged detail to the real line condition relies on operator observation and judgement. Discrepant exception details cause misassessment and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required supporting analytical records unavailable after attestation
rows.imp_rat: Supporting analytical records unavailable after attestation weakens defensibility (severity), but capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: EBR-stored records are retrievable, but supporting data in external systems can be unavailable at review. Electronic archival keeps records retrievable quickly after attestation. Centralized storage reduces reliance on analysts locating supporting records. Unavailable supporting records delay release and audits at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of post-attestation documentation changes lacking reviewer visibility
rows.imp_rat: Post-attestation changes lacking reviewer visibility are an integrity concern (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Locked records with audit trails reliably surface post-attestation changes to reviewers. Post-attestation change logging is automatic, requiring no analyst time. System versioning removes reliance on manual change notification. Visible post-attestation changes prevent costly hidden alterations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of corrected entries whose original value or correction history is obscured
rows.imp_rat: Obscured original values or correction history breaks audit reconstruction (severity), but capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: EBR audit trails reliably preserve original values and correction history, a core strength. Original values and history are retained automatically with no added time. Audit-trail retention removes reliance on analyst preserving history. Preserved correction history avoids costly integrity questions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a testing entry is changed by an unauthorized executor
rows.imp_rat: A testing entry changed by an unauthorized executor is an access-control breach (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access control is a mature, strong EBR feature that reliably blocks unauthorized editors. Role-based access enforcement is instantaneous with no analyst effort. Permission controls remove reliance on manual gatekeeping. Preventing unauthorized edits avoids costly integrity investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of corrected entries lacking executor or date attribution
rows.imp_rat: Corrected entries lacking executor or date attribution violate basic audit rules (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails reliably attach executor and date to corrections, a mature control. Executor and date are captured automatically at correction with no delay. System attribution removes any skill burden for annotating corrections. Reliable correction attribution avoids costly documentation gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition changes lacking performer attribution
rows.imp_rat: Condition changes lacking performer attribution weaken audit trail (severity), but this item is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: User-context and audit trails reliably attribute condition changes to a performer. Login-based attribution of condition changes is instantaneous. System attribution removes skill burden for naming the performer. Reliable performer attribution avoids costly documentation queries.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition entries misstating when the observation occurred
rows.imp_rat: Condition entries misstating observation timing distort the record (severity), kept below the ceiling as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Entry timestamps are reliable but may reflect recording time, not observation time, allowing mismatch. Automatic timestamping avoids manual time-of-observation entry. System capture reduces reliance on analyst recording when observations occurred. Wrong observation timing can mislead reviews at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of atypical-result records in which interpretation is indistinguishable from the original observation
rows.imp_rat: Interpretation indistinguishable from original observation blurs data integrity (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Free-text atypical documentation often mixes observation and interpretation, a persistent weakness. Separating observation from interpretation adds documentation time. Distinguishing raw observation from interpretation is a judgement-heavy skill task. Blended records complicate investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of atypical-result records missing context needed to interpret the observation
rows.imp_rat: Atypical-result records missing interpretive context harm reviewability (severity), but capped at 4.0 as a lower-ranked defect.
rows.sat_rat: Context capture for atypical results is unstructured and frequently incomplete. Adding interpretive context in the moment takes analyst time. Knowing what context to capture depends heavily on analyst expertise. Missing context forces investigation rework at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of atypical-result entries with metadata misrepresenting the event sequence
rows.imp_rat: Metadata misrepresenting event sequence undermines integrity (severity), but the ranking holds it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: System timestamps are reliable, but manual atypical entries can still misstate the event sequence. Auto-timestamped metadata avoids manual sequence documentation time. System metadata reduces reliance on analyst recording event order. Misrepresented event sequence undermines investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of tests initiated before their prerequisites are complete
rows.imp_rat: Tests initiated before prerequisites complete threaten data validity (severity), but capped at 4.0 as a lower-ranked item.
rows.sat_rat: Where prerequisite logic is configured it works, but many EBRs enforce it only partially. Prerequisite gating prevents time wasted on prematurely started tests. Automated prerequisite checks reduce reliance on analyst vigilance. Tests started before prerequisites can be wasted effort at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the wrong conditional path is followed after a result
rows.imp_rat: Following the wrong conditional path after a result can propagate errors (severity), but ranks lower and stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configured decision logic helps but complex or manual branches still allow wrong-path selection. Conditional branching in workflows routes automatically, saving decision time. Automated branching reduces reliance on analyst interpreting result-driven paths. A wrong conditional path can invalidate downstream work at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that testing resumes at the wrong step after an interruption
rows.imp_rat: Resuming testing at the wrong step after interruption risks invalid results (severity), but held at 4.0 by the ranking constraint.
rows.sat_rat: Persistent workflow state helps resume correctly, but interruptions still occasionally lead to wrong-step resumption. State-saving lets execution resume without re-doing steps, saving time. System-tracked position reduces reliance on analyst memory after interruptions. Resuming at the wrong step causes rework at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of analyses assigned a sequence conflicting with the approved specification
rows.imp_rat: Analyses sequenced against the approved specification risk validity (severity), but this is a lower-ranked defect capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configured workflows enforce approved order fairly reliably where built in, but ad hoc analyses can conflict. Specification-driven sequencing avoids time reordering analyses. Enforced order reduces reliance on analyst knowing the approved sequence. Sequence conflicts with specification can invalidate results at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested samples with missing or unreadable identity information
rows.imp_rat: Missing or unreadable sample identity information harms traceability (severity moderate), but as an ambiguity item it stays under the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic capture makes unreadable identity rare compared to paper, though missing entries still occur. Scanned identifiers are captured quickly and legibly, saving reconciliation time. Barcode capture removes the skill burden of transcribing identity. Unreadable identity forces re-sampling or investigation at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested samples with conflicting identity information across records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting identity information across records for tested samples affects traceability (severity), yet remains a lower-ranked instability item under 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: When integrated with LIMS/sample management identity is consistent, but cross-record conflicts persist where systems disconnect. Single sample-identity source reduces time reconciling conflicts. System-carried identity lowers reliance on analyst re-keying. Conflicting sample identities cause investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tested aliquots lacking traceability to their originating batch sample
rows.imp_rat: Aliquots lacking traceability to originating batch sample weakens data lineage (severity), kept below ceiling as a lower-ranked item.
rows.sat_rat: Aliquot-level traceability is often weakly supported, so links back to the originating sample can be lost. Aliquot traceability chains take time to maintain across steps. Maintaining aliquot-to-sample links requires analyst diligence. Loss of aliquot traceability can force re-testing at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sample-result associations changed without attribution
rows.imp_rat: Sample-result associations changed without attribution weaken audit integrity (severity), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails reliably attribute sample-result association changes when they occur in-system. Audit trails capture association changes automatically without extra time. System logging removes reliance on analyst documenting changes. Attributed association changes avoid costly disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a verified value is changed without renewed verification
rows.imp_rat: A verified value changed without renewed verification undermines control (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Some systems re-open verification on change, but this control is inconsistently implemented. Re-verification triggers add a controlled step but limit downstream time. Recognizing that a verified value changed relies partly on analyst awareness. Changing verified values without re-check risks costly errors.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterile fill-finish entries posted after scheduled checkpoints in each run
rows.imp_rat: Sterile fill-finish entries posted after checkpoints raise integrity concern (severity), but this delay item is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checkpoint prompts help, but entries still frequently post after their scheduled points. Fill-finish entries posted after checkpoints represent recurring timeliness slippage. Timely posting depends on operator discipline under production pressure. Late entries risk sterile-batch rework, a high-value cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between generation of required production data and its inclusion in the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Delay including generated production data into the batch record touches completeness (relevance), but as a timing item severity is low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Where interfaced, data lands promptly; where manual, it lags, giving mixed but improving performance. System integration shortens the lag between data generation and inclusion, though manual transfers still add latency. Automated data flow reduces reliance on staff to transcribe or import data. Latency ties up compilation resources but is a modest cost where integration exists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from production-run completion until the compiled batch record is inspection-ready
rows.imp_rat: Time to inspection-ready compiled record touches the compilation job (relevance), but as a delay its severity is low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: EBR speeds parts of the flow, but end-to-end inspection-readiness time is still driven by manual bottlenecks. Time from run completion to inspection-ready remains long due to sequential review and exception closure. Reaching inspection-ready depends on scarce reviewer and QA capacity. Long compilation cycles delay release and tie up working capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory expectations omitted from the assessment scope
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory expectations omitted from assessment scope has compliance severity, but is a lower-ranked scoping item capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Well-known regulations are usually captured, though niche or updated expectations are sometimes missed. Assembling applicable regulatory expectations from known sources is reasonably quick with existing libraries. Requires regulatory literacy but the core expectation set is well documented. Regulatory reference material is largely free and reusable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of regulated batch-record data and metadata lifecycle obligations omitted from validation requirements
rows.imp_rat: Omitting lifecycle obligations from validation requirements has compliance severity, but it is a validation-scoping concern ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Metadata lifecycle obligations are commonly under-specified, so omissions from validation requirements are a persistent gap. Enumerating every data and metadata lifecycle obligation is a lengthy manual exercise. Requires specialized understanding of metadata and record-lifecycle regulatory expectations that is scarce. Missed obligations trigger costly revalidation, though the assessment itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of regulated batch-data migration and reconciliation requirements omitted from validation scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting migration/reconciliation requirements from validation scope has compliance severity, but is a lower-ranked scoping item capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Migration and reconciliation requirements are frequently omitted from scope, a recurring and poorly mitigated weakness. Defining migration and reconciliation validation scope is a slow, detail-heavy task. Data-migration validation is a niche specialty many teams lack in depth. Missed migration requirements cause expensive data-integrity failures post-go-live.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of relevant batch-record action types omitted from traceability requirements
rows.imp_rat: Action types omitted from traceability requirements creates audit gaps (severity), but capped at 4.0 as a lower-ranked item.
rows.sat_rat: Action-type coverage is usually mostly complete but edge-case actions are periodically omitted. Listing all batch-record action types for traceability is a moderate effort. Requires process knowledge of every action a batch record undergoes. Omitted action types cause rework but the enumeration itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record actions lacking unambiguous originator attribution
rows.imp_rat: Actions lacking unambiguous originator attribution weaken accountability (severity), but held below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Unique-login attribution is a well-established, highly reliable control in compliant EBR systems. Automatic user attribution requires no extra time. Identity binding removes the need for manual attribution judgement. Attribution is inherent in authenticated systems at little added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record actions lacking consistent chronological context
rows.imp_rat: Actions lacking consistent chronological context undermines timeline integrity (severity), capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Chronological context via synchronized timestamps is a robust, standard capability, with only clock-sync edge cases remaining. System-generated timestamps are instantaneous. Automated timestamping removes manual effort entirely. Timestamping is built in with negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record actions from connected record sources lacking end-to-end lineage
rows.imp_rat: Connected-source actions lacking end-to-end lineage weakens traceability (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Within a single system lineage is good, but end-to-end across connected sources remains inconsistently achieved. Tracing end-to-end lineage across connected sources still requires manual stitching. Cross-system lineage demands integration expertise to establish and verify. Lineage gaps drive costly investigations across systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an MES certificate expiring before its replacement is activated
rows.imp_rat: An MES certificate expiring before replacement can interrupt secure connections, but it is a predictable operational-maintenance error of low centrality and moderate, recoverable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Where certificate inventories and reminders exist expiries are avoided, but untracked certificates still cause surprise outages. Renewal itself is quick once tracked, so time is a minor factor. Certificate management needs some PKI competence but is largely routine. Certificate tooling is inexpensive relative to the disruption an expiry causes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required MES manufacturing records omitted from the compliance archive
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required manufacturing records from the compliance archive carries regulatory risk (moderate severity) but is an archiving/administrative error only loosely tied to controlling production activities.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated firms follow established retention SOPs and archive validation, so most required records are captured, though edge-case tables and attachments still get missed. Compiling the full set of GxP manufacturing records into a compliant archive is a lengthy extraction and validation effort. Knowing which records are mandatory for retention requires records-management and regulatory expertise plus knowledge of the specific MES data model. Omissions surface during audits and can drive expensive remediation, but the archiving effort itself is a budgeted project cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch, production, and traceability records omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting batch and traceability records from an export undermines downstream data completeness with moderate compliance severity, but export completeness is a peripheral data-handling error.
rows.sat_rat: Export tooling is imperfect and referential links between batch, genealogy, and traceability data are frequently dropped, so omissions recur despite verification passes. Exporting complete batch, production, and traceability records is a slow, iterative process of query, extract, and verify. Identifying every required traceability linkage for export depends on scarce data-migration specialists familiar with the MES schema. Incomplete exports require rework or vendor assistance, adding cost, though the baseline export is part of the project budget.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required production, quality, and compliance records missing from the export or archive
rows.imp_rat: Missing required records from export or archive is a records-completeness error with moderate compliance stakes, but it is peripheral to actively measuring and controlling production.
rows.sat_rat: Retention procedures cover the common record types reasonably well, but quality and compliance metadata scattered across modules is often partially missed. Assembling production, quality, and compliance records for export or archive adds substantial elapsed time to the decommissioning schedule. Distinguishing which quality and compliance artifacts must be preserved requires QA and regulatory judgement that not every project team has on hand. Missing compliance records can trigger costly audit findings, though the extraction work is anticipated project spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of documented critical sterile fill-finish requirements omitted from the functional-fit assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile fill-finish requirements from a functional-fit assessment matters in regulated pharma contexts, but this selection-phase error is peripheral to day-to-day production control and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Functional-fit assessments routinely rely on generic checklists that under-represent niche sterile fill-finish needs, so documented requirements are frequently omitted. Compiling and cross-checking sterile fill-finish requirements against vendor functionality is a slow manual exercise done under acquisition deadlines. Enumerating every critical sterile fill-finish requirement requires deep aseptic-process and regulatory knowledge that few procurement teams have in-house. Omissions are cheap to make now but expensive later, and SMEs to prevent them are costly and scarce.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, IT managers, operations managers
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that shortlisted vendors cannot satisfy mandatory production, integration, data-integrity, and regulatory requirements
rows.imp_rat: Shortlisted vendors failing mandatory requirements is a procurement-stage error; its relevance to the operational control job is low and consequences, while real, are recoverable before commitment.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements-traceability matrices and vendor questionnaires catch many gaps, yet mandatory-requirement misses still surface late because vendor claims are hard to verify up front. RFI/RFP cycles take weeks but are a well-established sequence that buyers run routinely for MES selection. Correctly judging whether a vendor can truly satisfy mandatory pharma production, integration, and regulatory requirements demands scarce cross-domain expertise that most buying teams only partially possess. Structured RFPs and consultant support add cost but are a normalized line item in MES acquisition budgets.

rows.source_job: Configure data retention and archiving policies
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of production records being purged before mandated retention obligations end
rows.imp_rat: Purging production records before retention obligations end is a compliance/records error with regulatory consequences (moderate severity), but it is administrative and peripheral to real-time production control.
rows.sat_rat: Retention-lock and archival features reliably prevent premature purging when policies are correctly configured. Configuring retention and purge rules is a straightforward one-time setup task. Records-management and compliance staff routinely define retention policies. Long-term retention storage adds cost but is a planned compliance expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of valid process-data entries rejected during recording
rows.imp_rat: Valid process-data entries rejected during recording is rework of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Overly strict or misconfigured validation intermittently rejects valid data, a recurring nuisance. Valid entries wrongly rejected force re-entry, wasting time. Understanding and overriding false rejections depends on operator familiarity with validation rules. False rejections cause delay and workarounds but rarely product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing an in-progress entry after an access interruption
rows.imp_rat: Losing an in-progress entry after access interruption causes re-entry rework; modest stakes hold it low.
rows.sat_rat: Server-side session persistence makes losing an in-progress entry uncommon in mature EBRs. Auto-save and session recovery restore work quickly after interruptions in modern systems. Recovery features remove reliance on operators remembering to save. Lost in-progress entries force re-entry, a cost auto-save largely avoids.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of equipment-usage entries linked to the wrong batch step
rows.imp_rat: Equipment-usage entries linked to wrong batch step are traceability errors of modest scope; kept low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Step-scoped equipment entry makes wrong-step linkage fairly uncommon. Preset step context means correct equipment linkage costs no extra time. Contextual binding reduces operator need to select the correct step for equipment. Wrong-step equipment linkage causes traceability review cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in matching line equipment to its batch-record identifier
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity matching line equipment to its batch-record identifier is a traceability nuisance of modest impact; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Scanning resolves ambiguity where labels exist, but unlabeled or shared equipment leaves gaps. Matching physical equipment to its ID can slow entry when labels are unclear on the floor. Correct matching depends on operator familiarity with equipment tagging conventions. Ambiguous matching causes wrong IDs and traceability rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between material consumption and entry of its usage record
rows.imp_rat: Lag between material consumption and usage-record entry is a contemporaneity concern with modest stakes; peripheral to core compilation and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporaneous scanning is reliable where deployed, but manual steps allow lag. Point-of-use scanning shortens the gap, but manual consumption logging is often batched afterward. Prompt logging depends on operator discipline amid competing tasks. Delayed usage entries risk reconciliation errors and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material-usage records containing an incorrect unit of measure
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect unit of measure in usage records is a data-quality slip; often catchable, so both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Configured fixed units make incorrect unit-of-measure entries uncommon. Predefined units in fields save time versus free entry. Fixed unit-of-measure fields remove reliance on operators choosing units correctly. Wrong units corrupt reconciliation and can cause dosing errors, costly to resolve.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of initiated step records left incomplete after an interruption
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete step records after interruption leave gaps of moderate severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Open-step tracking helps, but interruptions still leave some records incomplete under load. Resuming an interrupted step is fast when the system holds partial state. Open-record indicators reduce reliance on operators recalling what was left unfinished. Incomplete step records cause holds and rework at review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of confidence loss after an interruption breaks the link between observed work and the entry being recorded
rows.imp_rat: Confidence loss when interruption breaks the work-to-entry link is a soft instability of modest severity; kept low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of corrected entries that remain incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Corrected entries remaining incomplete leave data gaps of moderate severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow prompts help, but abandoned or partial corrections still occur. Correction workflows can be left mid-process, and completing them takes deliberate time. Finishing a correction fully depends on operator understanding of the correction workflow. Incomplete corrections leave records in a nonconforming state, causing review cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity about the value that should replace an erroneous entry
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity about the replacement value for an erroneous entry is a modest correction-quality issue; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: The system offers no basis for the correct value, so ambiguity persists based on operator recall. Determining the right replacement value can require investigation time. Knowing the correct replacement value depends heavily on operator knowledge of what actually happened. Ambiguous replacement values risk wrong corrections and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of corrections that introduce another entry error
rows.imp_rat: Corrections introducing another error is compounding rework of modest severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validation on corrected values helps, but a correction can still introduce another mistake. Re-correcting an error adds time but is infrequent. Avoiding a new error during correction depends on operator care. Corrections introducing new errors compound rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in assigning an aseptic intervention to the applicable category
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity classifying an aseptic intervention category is a documentation-quality issue of moderate severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Predefined categories improve consistency, but ambiguous cases still cause misclassification. Category dropdowns speed classification but wrong picks need later correction. Assigning the correct intervention category depends heavily on operator training and judgement. Miscategorized interventions cause misassessment and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between occurrence of an execution exception and availability of its flag for assessment
rows.imp_rat: Delay in exception-flag availability for assessment is a timing lag with limited direct stakes for record compilation; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time flag propagation is fairly reliable, though raising the flag still depends on the operator. Electronic exception flags become visible to assessors near-instantly once raised. Automated routing reduces reliance on operators to manually escalate exceptions. Delayed exception visibility prolongs holds and costs; workflow routing shortens it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of exception flags lacking condition details needed for assessment
rows.imp_rat: Exception flags lacking condition details slow assessment; modest stakes and relevance keep it low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-field enforcement makes exception flags lacking needed detail fairly uncommon. Structured exception forms with mandatory fields make detail capture quick. Required-field prompts reduce reliance on operator knowing which condition details assessors need. Missing condition details delay assessment and cause back-and-forth cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of normal line conditions incorrectly flagged as exceptions during batch execution
rows.imp_rat: Normal conditions falsely flagged as exceptions create noise and rework of modest severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Guidance in forms helps, but over-flagging remains common with conservative operators. Flagging is fast, but false flags trigger unnecessary assessment time downstream. Distinguishing normal from exceptional conditions relies on operator expertise. False exception flags waste assessor effort and slow disposition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in classifying a flagged execution exception
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity classifying a flagged exception delays and confuses assessment; modest stakes keep it low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Structured categories help consistency, yet judgement-dependent classification still varies. Classification dropdowns are quick but misclassification requires downstream rework. Correctly classifying an exception relies on operator expertise and interpretation. Misclassified exceptions misroute assessment, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing an exception flag to an assessor without responsibility for the affected batch activity
rows.imp_rat: Routing an exception flag to a non-responsible assessor causes misrouting delay of modest severity; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow routing rules make wrong-assessor routing uncommon where configured. Automated routing rules deliver flags to the right assessor near-instantly. Configured routing removes reliance on operators knowing who owns the activity. Misrouted flags delay assessment and waste effort.

rows.source_job: Keep recording pace with the line
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production-line interruption while overdue batch documentation is completed
rows.imp_rat: Production-line interruption while overdue documentation completes is a delay of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Nothing in the status quo prevents documentation backlog from stopping the line, leaving this poorly addressed. Completing overdue documentation can halt the line, a direct throughput loss. Managing catch-up under a stopped line relies on operator prioritization. Line interruptions for documentation are directly costly in lost production.

rows.source_job: Keep recording pace with the line
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch steps whose entry workload exceeds the available recording window
rows.imp_rat: Entry workload exceeding the recording window is a capacity instability of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: EBR entry burden per step is often heavy and poorly matched to fast steps, leaving a persistent gap. When step entry workload exceeds the execution window, operators fall behind regardless of the tool. Balancing entry against the process window relies on operator prioritization skill. Exceeding the recording window forces catch-up that risks integrity findings.

rows.source_job: Keep recording pace with the line
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of overlapping line events that exceed the operator's recording capacity
rows.imp_rat: Overlapping line events exceeding operator recording capacity risks gaps of moderate severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Nothing in the status quo resolves concurrent-event overload; it depends on staffing. Overlapping events simply exceed one operator's recording bandwidth, and the EBR does not add throughput. Coping with simultaneous events demands operator multitasking skill the system does not offload. Overload forces deferred entry, risking data-integrity cost.

rows.source_job: Keep recording pace with the line
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of pending batch-record entries accumulated after a recording interruption
rows.imp_rat: Pending entries accumulated after interruption is a backlog delay of modest consequence; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Pending-item lists help track backlog but do not prevent accumulation under disruption. After interruptions, pending entries pile up and must be cleared, a real time backlog. Managing the backlog depends on operator memory of what still needs entry. Accumulated pending entries risk integrity findings and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity about the testing documentation required for completion attestation
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity about testing documentation required for attestation is a modest completeness risk; kept low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Completeness checklists help but ambiguity about required documentation persists. Uncertainty about required documentation slows the attestation decision. Knowing exactly what documentation completion requires depends on analyst expertise and SOP familiarity. Misjudging completeness causes costly rejected attestations or late findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between recording the final assigned result and attesting completion
rows.imp_rat: Delay between recording final result and attesting completion is a modest process lag; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow notifications improve timeliness, though queuing delays persist. Electronic routing shortens the gap between final result and attestation versus paper hand-offs. Attestation timing depends on signer availability rather than skill. Delays here extend cycle time and hold inventory at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between test completion and result recording
rows.imp_rat: Delay between test completion and result recording is a modest timeliness issue; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporaneous entry is common practice, improving timeliness reliably where interfaced. Direct electronic recording shortens the gap between test completion and result entry versus paper transcription. Fast recording depends less on skill where instrument capture exists. Delayed recording risks memory-based errors and rework at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of completed results rejected for incompatibility with the required record structure
rows.imp_rat: Completed results rejected for record-structure incompatibility cause re-entry of modest severity; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Input validation reliably enforces structure, though rigid templates sometimes reject legitimate results. Structured validation rejects incompatible entries at input, saving later rework time. Field validation reduces reliance on analyst formatting correctly. Rejected results cause re-entry effort but prevent bigger downstream costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between test execution and compilation of its analytical result
rows.imp_rat: Delay compiling an analytical result after execution is a throughput lag of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Where interfaced, timely compilation is reliable; manual transfers still introduce delay. Instrument interfacing can compile analytical results near-immediately after execution. Automated compilation reduces the manual effort that would otherwise slow it. Delayed compilation ties up review capacity at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of corrected entries with a missing or ambiguous rationale
rows.imp_rat: Corrected entries with missing or ambiguous rationale is a modest audit-trail gap; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Required reason-for-change is standard, though free-text rationales can still be ambiguous. Mandatory reason-for-change prompts add minimal time at correction. Forced rationale fields reduce reliance on analyst remembering to explain. Missing rationale triggers costly follow-up queries.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time an identified entry error remains unresolved
rows.imp_rat: An identified entry error left unresolved is a modest timeliness risk; kept low under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Error flagging is prompt, but the resolution workflow is not fast enough to prevent lingering open items. Flagged errors are visible quickly, but resolution still waits on investigation and reviewer availability. Resolving errors depends on analyst and QA expertise and bandwidth. Unresolved errors hold batches and consume resources at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting corrections to the same entry
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting corrections to the same entry cause confusion and rework of modest severity; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Locking and versioning limit conflicting corrections, though not all systems enforce it strictly. Record locking during edits reduces time spent resolving conflicting corrections. Concurrency controls reduce reliance on coordination between analysts. Conflicting corrections cause confusion and rework at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between observing test conditions and documenting them
rows.imp_rat: Delay documenting observed test conditions is a contemporaneity lag of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporaneous entry helps consistency but is not enforced, so lags occur. Real-time condition entry reduces observe-to-document lag when done at the bench. Timeliness depends on analyst habit rather than system prompting. Late condition recording risks reconstruction errors at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity about which testing conditions require documentation
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity about which testing conditions require documentation is a scoping imprecision of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Templates imply some requirements but do not fully remove ambiguity about what must be documented. Ambiguity about which conditions to document slows the analyst deciding at execution. Determining required conditions leans on analyst training rather than clear system guidance. Guessing wrong causes rework or investigation at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition values lacking unambiguous units or reference bases
rows.imp_rat: Condition values lacking clear units or reference bases create ambiguity of modest severity; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Templated units help, but free-entry condition fields still allow ambiguous or missing reference bases. Predefined unit fields save time versus specifying reference bases manually. Fixed units and bases reduce reliance on analyst knowledge. Ambiguous condition units can cause misinterpretation at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between observing an atypical result and documenting it
rows.imp_rat: Delay documenting an atypical result risks recall accuracy of modest severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Timely capture improves accuracy, but no strong enforcement guarantees immediacy. Contemporaneous EBR entry shortens the gap between observing and documenting atypical results, though busy workflows still cause delays. Prompt documentation depends on analyst discipline rather than automation. Delayed atypical documentation can weaken investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among instructions defining the prescribed testing order
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting instructions on testing order create ambiguity of moderate severity; kept low under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Instruction conflicts stem from authoring/change-control gaps the EBR does not fully resolve. Consolidated electronic instructions reduce time reconciling conflicting orders. A single authoritative sequence lessens reliance on analyst interpretation. Conflicting order instructions cause confusion and rework at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that repeat testing is inserted at the wrong sequence point
rows.imp_rat: Repeat testing at the wrong sequence point affects data validity modestly; relevance and severity kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Repeat/re-test insertion is often loosely controlled, leaving real risk of wrong-sequence placement. Inserting repeat testing at the right point can require slow manual steps. Correctly placing repeat testing in sequence needs analyst expertise. Wrongly inserted repeats can distort sequence integrity at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between result recording and completion of accuracy verification
rows.imp_rat: Delay completing accuracy verification of a recorded result is a modest timeliness concern; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Structured verification routing helps, but reviewer bottlenecks still delay closure. Verification workflows can queue, extending the gap between recording and verified accuracy. Verification speed depends on reviewer availability and expertise. Verification backlogs delay release, tying up resources at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reviewers suspect concealed or manipulated changes when correction history is unclear
rows.imp_rat: Reviewer suspicion of concealed changes when history is unclear is a trust concern of modest severity; kept low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disposition holds caused by unclear decision ownership or approval status
rows.imp_rat: Disposition holds from unclear ownership delay release but have modest direct stakes for record structure; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Configured EBR workflows with defined roles and escalation make ownership clear in most cases, though absent approvers and undefined edge-case routing still cause occasional stalls. Electronic routing and status dashboards cut hold time from unclear ownership by showing exactly whose approval is pending. Little specialized skill is needed; the workflow explicitly assigns responsibility, minimizing judgement about who acts next. Reduced approval-ambiguity holds lower the carrying cost of batches stalled in queue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that compiled batch records support disposition
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that compiled records support disposition is a reassurance-timing concern of modest severity; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of lost reassurance when manufacturing and testing evidence conflicts
rows.imp_rat: Lost reassurance when manufacturing and testing evidence conflicts is a soft trust issue of modest severity; kept low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of confidence erosion when consequential record changes surface near disposition sign-off
rows.imp_rat: Confidence erosion when changes surface near sign-off is a soft instability of modest severity; kept low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that reconciled batch evidence can withstand inspection scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty about withstanding inspection is a soft reassurance issue of modest severity; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of missing a stated inspection-readiness commitment for reviewed batch records
rows.imp_rat: Missing an inspection-readiness commitment is a schedule delay of modest severity; capped low under the ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that documentation demand exceeds available qualified workforce capacity during concurrent sterile fill-finish runs
rows.imp_rat: Documentation demand exceeding qualified workforce during concurrent runs is a capacity strain of modest severity; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity planning is largely manual and reactive, so demand routinely exceeds qualified workforce with little buffer. Capacity shortfalls during concurrent sterile runs create backlogs that stretch timelines. The core problem is scarcity of qualified documentation staff, which no tool creates on demand. Overload forces overtime, temporary staffing, and error-driven rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of compilation delay caused by correcting late-discovered record errors
rows.imp_rat: Compilation delay from correcting late-discovered errors is rework of modest severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Errors discovered late are inherent to sequential review and remain a common source of delay. Correcting late-discovered errors is a direct, recurring cause of compilation delay. Late error correction demands skilled investigation to fix without introducing new errors. Rework of late errors consumes staff time and delays release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of overdue batch-record actions across overlapping production runs
rows.imp_rat: Overdue actions across overlapping runs is a workload delay with modest direct impact on record structure; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Dashboards list overdue items but do not resolve the capacity crunch driving them. Overdue actions accumulate across overlapping runs, a persistent timeliness failure. Clearing overdue actions competes for the same limited qualified staff. Overdue actions cause overtime and delayed release costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch records awaiting required review or approval during compilation
rows.imp_rat: Records awaiting review during compilation is a throughput delay of modest consequence; kept under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow visibility exists but does not clear the queues, so waiting persists. Records waiting in review/approval queues is a chronic delay in compilation. Queue times reflect scarcity of qualified reviewers. Idle records in queues extend cycle time and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compiled batch records reopened after late source-data changes
rows.imp_rat: Reopening compiled records after late source-data changes is rework of moderate severity; held under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Late source-data changes still propagate into reopened records fairly often despite change controls. Reopening compiled records after late source-data changes forces slow re-review cycles. Assessing the impact of late changes needs experienced judgement. Reopening records wastes prior review effort, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch-record compilation blocked by unresolved deviation decisions
rows.imp_rat: Compilation blocked by unresolved deviation decisions is a throughput delay; relevance and severity are modest and kept under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tracking shows the block but does nothing to speed the underlying decision. Compilation blocked awaiting deviation decisions is a direct, recurring source of delay. Deviation decisions require scarce SME/QA judgement that gates the timeline. Blocked compilation extends cycle time and delays release revenue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a conforming batch-record management approach is judged nonconforming
rows.imp_rat: Judging a conforming approach nonconforming causes unnecessary rework of modest severity; kept low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Subjective criteria make false-negative judgements fairly common, though slightly less damaging than false positives. Over-cautious re-review of falsely flagged approaches adds delay. Correctly clearing a conforming approach needs confident expert interpretation. False rejections waste effort and can needlessly disqualify viable systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of regulated interfaces and system boundaries excluded from validation scope
rows.imp_rat: Interfaces excluded from validation scope is a coverage gap of moderate severity but indirect relevance; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Interfaces and system boundaries are commonly under-scoped, leaving integration risks unvalidated. Cataloguing interfaces and boundaries for scope takes meaningful time. Requires integration architecture knowledge alongside validation expertise. Excluded interfaces surface as costly late findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validation acceptance criteria permitting subjective interpretation
rows.imp_rat: Subjective validation acceptance criteria weaken rigor modestly; both proxies kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria frequently permit subjective interpretation, undermining consistent validation outcomes. Rewriting criteria to be objective adds upfront authoring time. Writing unambiguous acceptance criteria is a craft skill many authors lack. Subjective criteria cause disputes and reruns that raise cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of audit-traceability criteria not objectively verifiable during validation
rows.imp_rat: Non-objectively-verifiable audit-traceability criteria is a validation-quality gap of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Audit-traceability criteria are often stated in ways that cannot be objectively verified, a common residual gap. Making audit-trace criteria objectively verifiable adds specification effort. Requires knowing what evidence is objectively demonstrable during testing. Non-verifiable criteria trigger costly retesting and audit challenges.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of EBR functions omitted from the data-integrity risk assessment
rows.imp_rat: EBR functions omitted from data-integrity risk assessment is a planning gap; relevance to daily compilation is indirect and severity is deferred, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Risk assessments are standard practice yet functions are regularly overlooked, leaving unassessed pockets. Mapping all EBR functions into a risk assessment is time-consuming but bounded. Comprehensive function inventory demands both system and process knowledge combined. Function-level risk assessment is labor-intensive but uses standard templates.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of EBR function dependencies omitted from validation-effort decisions
rows.imp_rat: Function dependencies omitted from validation decisions is a coverage gap of modest severity; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Dependencies are frequently overlooked in effort decisions, weakening validation coverage. Mapping function dependencies extends analysis time. Understanding cross-function dependencies demands strong system architecture insight. Ignored dependencies cause misjudged effort and later cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of high-risk EBR functions assigned insufficient validation effort
rows.imp_rat: High-risk EBR functions with insufficient validation effort is a coverage gap of moderate severity; kept low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based validation is established practice, yet high-risk functions still sometimes receive insufficient effort. Risk-based effort allocation frameworks speed prioritization once applied. Judging sufficient effort for high-risk functions needs experienced validation leads. Under-resourcing high-risk functions risks expensive rework, but frameworks curb misallocation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in linking function risk to specified validation effort
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity linking function risk to validation effort is a planning imprecision of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: The link between risk and effort remains ambiguous in most methodologies, producing inconsistent outcomes. Debating the risk-to-effort linkage consumes review cycles. Translating function risk into concrete validation effort is highly interpretive and expertise-dependent. Ambiguity leads to either wasted or insufficient effort, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recommended compliance positions lacking rationale traceable to applicable regulatory sources
rows.imp_rat: Compliance positions lacking traceable rationale weaken defensibility modestly; both proxies kept low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Rationale traceability is inconsistently maintained, so many positions rest on tacit judgement rather than cited sources. Documenting rationale traceable to sources for every position is laborious and slows recommendations. Producing defensible source-traceable rationale requires senior regulatory reasoning that few possess. Undocumented rationale creates expensive audit exposure and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of departed or transferred personnel retaining active MES access
rows.imp_rat: Departed personnel retaining access is a real security exposure, but access revocation is administrative and not central to measuring/controlling production; consequences moderate, ranked below prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Joiner-mover-leaver processes handle most cases, but stale active access for departed staff still slips through. Deprovisioning can lag behind HR events when the process is manual. Basic access administration is routine and needs little specialized skill. Identity tooling is standard and inexpensive at scale.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of slowdown in critical batch-execution transactions after database optimization
rows.imp_rat: Slowdown of critical batch-execution transactions after DB optimization can affect responsiveness, moderately relevant to control, but as a post-tuning instability it ranks below the prior batch and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Optimizations are usually validated, but occasional plan changes still slow critical batch transactions. Database optimization normally improves transaction speed, and regressions are the exception. Tuning and validating post-optimization performance requires DBA expertise. Performance testing infrastructure adds cost but is generally justified.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that adjusted MES capacity remains insufficient during peak sterile fill-finish production
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient adjusted capacity during peak sterile fill-finish could constrain throughput, a fairly severe scenario, yet it is a sizing/instability edge case ranked below the prior batch, so held at the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity planning usually holds, but unanticipated peaks occasionally expose insufficient headroom. Capacity adjustments take planning time but are typically provisioned ahead of peaks. Sizing capacity for peak sterile fill-finish load requires performance-engineering expertise. Over-provisioning to guarantee headroom costs money that constrains sizing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between upgraded modules and existing MES configurations and interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between upgraded modules and existing configs/interfaces can disrupt operation, moderately relevant to continued control, but as an upgrade-instability item it sits below the prior batch and is capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Upgrades frequently surface conflicts with custom configurations and interfaces that testing failed to predict. Resolving module-configuration conflicts extends upgrade windows considerably. Anticipating conflicts between upgraded modules and existing interfaces demands deep system knowledge. Thorough compatibility testing is expensive and often trimmed.

rows.source_job: Inspect system configuration for unauthorized changes
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production-critical MES settings excluded from configuration inspection
rows.imp_rat: Excluding production-critical settings from configuration inspection can allow drift, but it is a governance/QA check tangential to the moment-to-moment job of measuring and controlling production; consequences are moderate and containable.
rows.sat_rat: Structured inspections cover core settings, yet some critical settings are routinely excluded from scope. Configuration inspections take time, especially when settings are numerous. Knowing which production-critical settings to inspect depends on experienced engineers. Inspection tooling exists but comprehensive coverage costs effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of in-scope MES records omitted from a required retention hold
rows.imp_rat: Omitting in-scope records from a retention hold is a legal-hold compliance error with real regulatory exposure but very low relevance to the operational task of controlling production.
rows.sat_rat: Retention holds are generally reliable, but records occasionally fall outside the hold scope and get omitted. Applying holds is quick once scope is defined. Correctly scoping which records must be held requires compliance judgement. Hold tooling is established, keeping incremental cost modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of retained MES records that authorized users cannot retrieve without the retired application
rows.imp_rat: Failure to retrieve retained records without the retired application affects long-term data access and audit readiness, but it is a decommissioning-era concern peripheral to running live production control; capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Long-term readable archiving in a vendor-neutral, human-legible form is inconsistently achieved, so users often cannot retrieve records without the original application. Retrieving retained records without the retired application depends on how archives were rendered, and ad hoc retrieval can be very slow. Accessing legacy data absent its native application often requires IT specialists to reconstruct or interpret raw exports, a demanding task. Firms frequently must keep the old system on life-support or buy archival readers, incurring ongoing licensing and infrastructure cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a live interface remains connected after MES retirement
rows.imp_rat: A live interface left connected after retirement is a decommissioning risk with potential for stray data flow, but low centrality to the operational control job; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Interface inventories are often incomplete, so a stray connection remaining active after retirement is a common, recurring gap. Identifying and disconnecting every interface takes time given the many integrations MES typically has with ERP, SCADA, LIMS, and equipment. Discovering undocumented or forgotten interfaces requires integration architects who understand the full interface inventory. A lingering live interface can cause data leakage or orphaned processes, but the cleanup itself is low direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of MES accounts and credentials omitted from the revocation scope
rows.imp_rat: Accounts omitted from revocation scope is a security-completeness error with exposure risk, but low centrality to measuring/controlling production; held low.
rows.sat_rat: Local or service accounts outside central identity management are frequently overlooked, so a portion of credentials escapes the revocation scope. Revoking MES accounts and credentials is fast where identity management is centralized. Deprovisioning is routine IAM work requiring little specialized skill when a directory integration exists. Credential revocation is nearly free using existing identity governance tooling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of claimed MES compliance support lacking sufficient validation evidence
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient validation evidence behind compliance claims risks audit findings, yet it is a documentation/assurance error peripheral to the core execution job; severity limited by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors commonly assert compliance support without attaching sufficient validation evidence, and buyers accept a meaningful share unverified. Requesting and reviewing validation evidence lengthens the evaluation but is a familiar step. Distinguishing genuine validation evidence from marketing compliance claims requires seasoned CSV/quality reviewers who are in short supply. Reviewing evidence costs auditor time, far cheaper than remediating unsupported claims post-purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required data-integrity, data-protection, and sterile-manufacturing obligations omitted from the MES contract
rows.imp_rat: Omitting data-integrity and sterile-manufacturing obligations from the MES contract is a procurement/legal error; it carries compliance risk but is minimally central to the software's production-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Standard clause libraries and quality-agreement templates cover common obligations, but sterile-specific ones are still routinely omitted from MES contracts. Legal and quality contract reviews add weeks but follow an established negotiation cadence. Translating data-integrity, data-protection, and sterile-manufacturing obligations into enforceable contract clauses needs combined legal and GxP expertise that is hard to assemble. Contract-drafting effort is a modest, budgeted cost relative to the exposure it addresses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required MES security and regulatory evidence unavailable for review
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable security/regulatory evidence for review threatens audit posture but is a compliance-artifact error peripheral to production execution; kept low per ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Mature vendors maintain audit packages and SOC-type reports, but portions of requested evidence remain unavailable or under NDA at review time. Security and regulatory evidence requests add turnaround delay awaiting vendor document packages. Knowing which security and regulatory artifacts to demand and how to assess them requires specialized audit skill. Evidence review is inexpensive relative to the risk of missing security gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the trial environment differs from sterile fill-finish production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Trial environment diverging from sterile production undermines validation confidence, but it is a test-fidelity concern peripheral to live control; severity moderate, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Trials almost always differ materially from real sterile fill-finish conditions, making this a chronically under-served evaluation gap. Standing up a representative trial environment is time-consuming and often truncated to fit acquisition schedules. Configuring a trial that mirrors sterile fill-finish production conditions demands rare engineering and process expertise. Building production-faithful trial environments is expensive, so most buyers settle for cheaper generic demos.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reviewed vendor evidence that does not represent sterile fill-finish production and regulatory conditions
rows.imp_rat: Vendor evidence not representing sterile production/regulatory conditions weakens qualification assurance, a procurement-review defect peripheral to the execution job; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors supply generic or non-sterile reference evidence and buyers frequently accept it as representative, a persistent weakness. Vetting whether vendor evidence reflects sterile conditions adds review time that is often cut short. Recognizing that reference cases and evidence do not represent sterile fill-finish regulatory contexts requires deep domain judgement. Accepting non-representative evidence is cheap now but costly when it misleads the decision.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical MES workflow steps performed incorrectly during operator competency assessment
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly performed workflow steps during competency assessment reflect training-validation gaps, moderately relevant to safe control, with real but bounded severity under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Competency assessments are inconsistent, so critical workflow steps are still performed incorrectly at meaningful rates during evaluation. Operator competency assessment on MES workflows is a slow, individualized process. Reducing incorrectly performed steps depends heavily on trainer quality and operator aptitude, which vary widely. Training time and assessment labor represent a recurring cost per operator.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of MES accounts assigned roles granting access beyond approved job responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Over-privileged accounts are a security-hygiene error with potential for misuse, but role scope has low centrality to executing production control itself; kept low per ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Role templates reduce error, but least-privilege drift still leaves accounts over-provisioned fairly often. Reviewing role assignments against job responsibilities is a recurring, time-consuming exercise.
